posts.caption:
When photographer @agnesvita went to Menorca to develop a personal project, she did so as a practice of stripping back the architecture of her everyday life, something she says felt necessary for survival. ⁠
⁠
“I don't think I was trying to escape. I was trying to find something,” she says, explaining how the trip came following a period of postnatal depression. Travelling solo as a mother might have felt controversial to some in her life, but for the photographer it was essential “to be able to listen to my own thoughts,” she explains to @laurahavlin. ⁠
⁠
As photographer Agnes Lloyd-Platt debuts her solo show Make Me Saline at @monument_london, she explains the stripped-back process that transmuted pain into hope. Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Agnes Lloyd-Platt: Make Me Saline. Photography by Agnes Lloyd-Platt. Courtesy of @monument_london
posts.alt_text: Photo by AnOther Magazine on May 21, 2026. May be a black-and-white image of one or more people, people standing, poster and text that says 'AnOther Agnes Lloyd Lloyd-Platt's Platt's New Exhibition is a Beautiful Portrait of Survival'.

⁠
Read @rurydoherty's review at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
🎥/📸 The Man I Love, 2026⁠
⁠
#TheManILove
posts.alt_text: Photo by AnOther Magazine on May 21, 2026. May be an image of bathtub, shower and text that says 'Performance seeps into nearly every scene of The Man I Love, Ira Sachs' frank romantic drama about the devastating impact of HIV/ AIDS on New York's gay community and downtown theatre scene. Our subject is Jimmy (Rami Malek), a minor, localised theatre celebrity on the mend from a debilitating bout of AIDS-related illness, cared for by his non- performer partner, Dennis (Tom Sturridge). He's excited about his new starring role to focus on. But outside of rehearsals, performance nance is the principle method of processing or delaying the loss that threatens to consume Jimmy's world.'.

posts.caption:
Around five years ago, New York-based Dutch photographer @pieterhenket was frustrated with the negativity he saw directed at young people. “I would hear from my older friends that the younger generation weren’t doing anything interesting,” he says. ⁠
⁠
Assembling a diverse group of queer young people cast from streets, parks, agencies, through connections and word of mouth, Henket recruited Mexico City-born stylist and Willy Chavarria collaborator @chino.castilla to help bring his nonconformist vision to life. ⁠
⁠
The result is a series of delineated black-and-white portraits with costuming that blends recognisable Mexican symbols – serapes, football kits, a Luchador mask – with operatic flare in the shape of horns, balloons, chaps, corsets and outsized silhouettes. ⁠
⁠
@ananda_pellerin speaks with Henket about the resulting photo book published by @damiani_books at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Birds of Mexico City by Pieter Henket. © Pieter Henket. Courtesy of and published by Damiani Books ⁠
1. La Mujer, from the series Birds of Mexico City, 2021⁠

⁠

posts.caption:
Since rising to prominence in the 1970s, photographer @sarahmoon_official has enchanted audiences with her ethereal work, which has a painterly and abstract texture that renders her images dreamlike and a little ghostly. ⁠
⁠
“Sarah believes strongly in the atmosphere of a photograph or picture, rather than simply documentation,” says her gallerist @michaelhoppengallery, who has worked with Moon for 37 years. Open now, the gallery presents a new exhibition of her work spanning 2003 to the present, the fifth solo show in a nearly four-decade collaboration. ⁠
⁠
“That is what drives my creative energy, knowing there is something ethereal, something different in the air,” Moon tells @laurahavlin. Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Photography by #SarahMoon. Courtesy of Michael Hoppen Gallery⁠
1. Yue pour Yohji Yamamoto, 2019⁠

posts.caption:
One hundred years since her birth (on 1 June, 1926), Marilyn Monroe remains one of the most mythologised and photographed figures of the 20th century. Yet, of all the raked-over facets of her life, the idea of Marilyn Monroe as an avid reader is one crucial but less-explored aspect of her legacy. ⁠
⁠
Inspired by a list of Monroe’s library in her estate sale, @crowther_gail’s new book, Marilyn and Her Books, takes a look at the legendary actress’s life through the prism of the literature she consumed. “I have always been fascinated by other people’s bookshelves and what they can say about a person, so I immediately wanted to engage in an in-depth shelf-snoop of Marilyn’s library to see what that would uncover,” Crowther tells @ohnoitsemilyd. ⁠
⁠
Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 #MarilynMonroe reading Ulysses by James Joyce, Long Island, 1955 © Eve Arnold

posts.caption:
On 20 May, @louisvuitton Cruise 2027 show presented by @nicolasghesquiere took place at The Frick Collection in Manhattan, New York City.
The collection began with a Louis Vuitton vintage leather suitcase from the 1930s, featuring a marker drawing by Keith Haring in 1984. Keith Haring is an artist known for symbolising New York’s street culture and pop art through graffiti. In this show, his works were expressed through prints throughout the collection — from graphic T-shirts to bags.
The show reimagined moments that transcend time in New York, brought together through the classical space of The Frick Collection, Keith Haring’s art, and Louis Vuitton’s contemporary fashion. Swipe right to see Keith Haring’s art in the LV collection.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
‘Taiwan Travelogue’, a novel originally written in Mandarin Chinese by Yáng Shuāng-zǐ and translated by Lin King (@linkinglionking) has become the winner of 2026 International Booker Prize. The duo is the first Taiwanese and Taiwanese-American winners of the prize. The novel, which takes the form of a fictional translation of a rediscovered Japanese travel memoir, explores history, power and love through the lens of two women’s culinary tour across 1930s Japan-controlled Taiwan. The £50,000 prize will be split equally between the writers.
📸: Getty Images and Graywolf Press
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
Over her stellar 20-year career, @phoebejtonkin has captivated audiences. The Sydney-born, New York-based actor began acting as a teenager playing a mermaid in the series ‘H2O: Just Add Water,’ and has since achieved a dynamic array of dramatic parts, a stint in the director’s chair and a new role producing and starring in the adaptation of crime thriller, ‘The Dark Lake’. While she’s relishing the collaborative process, the actor and May 2026 cover star tells BAZAAR, acting remains her first love.“I feel really lucky to be invited into the collaborative process so early on in a project. Usually, as an actor, you turn up and you’re told, ‘This is what your set looks like, these are your fellow actors’. And with this, I get to be there from the inception of the project and be part of these decisions.”
Through the link in bio, Phoebe Tonkin reflects on her career and attraction to robust roles that “lean into the complexities of being a woman.”
Phoebe Tonkin wears @tiffanyandco HardWear collection for the May 2026 issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
“I’m attracted to things that are authentic and grounded,” @phoebejtonkin tells BAZAAR of her latest role in the cross-genre ‘Two Years Later’, a post-pandemic romantic dramedy set in Brisbane. Tonkin plays Emily, a woman with a complicated love and family life who sets out on a series of dates with Ryan, played by Brenton Thwaites, to work out if they’re destined to marry. “There was something quite relatable and ordinary about these two people who are in their 30s and trying to navigate dating, coming out of a pandemic, all the isolation and loneliness that they’ve been dealing with. And I just thought it was really real,” she shares. It was this funny, quirky love story drama that you don’t get to do often.”
Continue reading Phoebe Tonkin’s cover story through the link in bio.

posts.caption:
After two months of discussion between Estée Lauder Companies and Puig about a possible merger, the global beauty conglomerates are walking away.
On May 21, the companies both released announcements that their discussions have been terminated and they will remain independent. The news comes two days after a report in the Spanish newspaper Expansión that Charlotte Tilbury was seeking a renegotiation of her contract, potentially complicating the deal.
Stéphane de La Faverie, president and chief executive officer of The Estée Lauder Companies, said in a statement, “We are grateful for the conversations we have had with Puig,” and expressed optimism in ELC’s future as a standalone company. “We are more optimistic than ever about our ability to unlock significant long-term value through Beauty Reimagined, and we remain focused on accelerating that progress.”
Read the full story by BoF’s @liz_flora #linkinbio

posts.caption:
AI has made it easier than ever for creators to circulate their ideas of what a brand’s next release should look like. While these mockups help build hype, they can leave shoppers confused and disappointed by the real thing.
The situation poses a complex challenge for an industry defined by tightly controlled image-building and carefully orchestrated rollouts for new products, campaigns and collaborations.
BoF’s Lei Takanashi (@leitakanashi) explores how brands can navigate AI-generated mockups — balancing hype, control, and trust as fan-made visuals blur the line between reality and expectation. Read more #linkinbio

posts.caption:
Fandom is the new commercial engine.
As digital subcultures decentralise influence, the industry is shifting away from selling a lifestyle towards building highly participatory communities of brand fans.
From the rise of serialised short-form narratives to early talent identification — as seen with Gap x Katseye — cultural relevance on TikTok is now earned through active participation.
To unpack how fashion, beauty and publishers can unlock the full potential of the platform, our latest knowledge report — produced in partnership with @tiktok — features exclusive data and strategic frameworks from leading industry voices, brands and publishers, including @ly.as of @lawatchparty, @omondi of @thecuttingroomfloor, Shahbaz Khan of @nfl, @charlgeoghegan of @refybeauty, @1hyrum of @adanola, @marinations of Kyra and James Stafford, global head of operations at TikTok.

posts.caption:
After more than doubling its stock value in the last year, Victoria’s Secret’s return to its glamorous, sexy roots seems to be working out. Next month, it’s making the claim official.
The lingerie giant announced Thursday an update to its stock ticker, a short unique abbreviation to identify a publicly traded company, from VSCO to VSXY. The move that will take effect on June 2. It’s a seemingly small change — just two letters — but according to chief executive Hillary Super, it solidifies the evolution that’s been in progress since she joined the company in late 2024.
BoF's Diana Pearl (@dianaspearl) spoke to Super about reviving Victoria's Secret by returning to its core identity. Read more #linkinbio

posts.caption:
In May, sleeping bags lined pavements and police barriers went up outside Swatch stores from Times Square to Dubai. The object of this global hysteria was not a piece of high-end mechanical art, but the “Royal Pop” — a $400 pocket watch collaboration between mass-market giant Swatch and watchmaker Audemars Piguet. Based on AP’s iconic Royal Oak, which typically starts at $20,000, the launch divided the insular watch enthusiast community while captivating Gen-Z consumers and equity analysts alike.
In this episode of The Debrief, senior correspondent Sheena Butler-Young (@sheena.butler.young) is joined by retail editor Cathaleen Chen (@kittykewlcat) and luxury editor Mimosa Spencer (@mimosaspencer) to evaluate the highs and lows of the viral launch, the operational chaos across retail and whether a plastic pendant can truly serve as a long-term customer recruitment tool.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheDebriefPodcast
📷 Getty Images

posts.caption:
Marquee Brands is acquiring Roberto Cavalli (@roberto_cavalli), the brand management company said, without disclosing financial terms.
Marquee Brands will buy a majority stake in Cavalli, while the seller, Damac Group — a conglomerate owned by property tycoon Hussain Sajwani — will retain a minority interest.
Cavalli will join Marquee Brands’ stable of 21 labels spanning fashion, lifestyle, home and culinary names, including Body Glove, Bruno Magli and Martha Stewart.
Read more the acquisition, by BoF's Eric Sylvers #linkinbio

posts.caption: louis vuitton resort 2027 felt like walking through new york and seeing five completely different women on the same street. keith haring energy everywhere, vintage ruffles, sporty layers, leather jackets, and playful accessories . the whole collection felt like a love letter to old and new at once. every look felt like a different character. it’s trippy, dramatic, and weirdly believable. this honestly felt like a really strong continuation of his last collection. the references are almost overwhelming at times, but that’s exactly what makes his work feel alive. the best part is that underneath all the art references and surreal styling, the collection still felt incredibly wearable. there’s something really refreshing about watching a designer fully commit to his own world without trying to water it down or make it more “marketable.” nicolas ghesquière fully trusting his own instincts, not trying to simplify himself for anyone is a treat to watch, a luxury creatives can only dream of.

posts.caption:
At the Frick Museum on Wednesday night, @louisvuitton introduced François Boucher’s putti to Keith Haring’s radiant babies.
For the label’s Cruise 2027 collection, staged in the hallowed halls of the @frickcollection a New York City Gilded Age mansion-turned-art gallery, designer @nicolasghesquiere created several pieces adorned with works by the late US pop artist Keith Haring.
Haring’s trademark faceless figures dominated satin tops, dresses, bags, leather jackets and high-top sneakers. “Pop art, pop culture and pop luxury,” read the brand’s accompanying press release. “The notion of popular as a powerful medium, the conveyance of messages to all.”
🎥: CNN
📸 : Louis Vuitton
posts.alt_text: Photo by CNN Style on May 21, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'ฺ YOR ሳ CNN'.

posts.caption:
At @festivaldecannes on Wednesday, supermodel @bellahadid attended the premiere of Antonin Baudry's new Charles de Gaulle biopic, "La Bataille de Gaulle," in a form-fitting ivory gown by Schiaparelli.
From its trompe l'œil lace embroidery to the dark brooch holding everything together, the French couture house's inspiration was clear: the crochet floral gown that late style icon Jane Birkin wore to the Union of the Artists gala in Paris in 1969.
In contrast to Hadid's picture-perfect styling, Birkin's outfit had resulted from a last-minute wardrobe fix — one that epitomized her gloriously blithe approach to fashion.
Believing her neckline to be too high, the English socialite decided to wear her @emiliopucci dress backwards. In doing so, she turned a backless gown into a daringly plunging one.
Almost six decades later, moments of such sartorial insouciance have all but disappeared from the red carpet. But let this not be a criticism of Hadid, her longtime stylist mimicuttrell or the gown's creator, @schiaparelli creative director @danielroseberry, who together created an outfit befitting of the moment.
📸 : Lyvans Boolaky/Getty Images/Samir Hussein/WireImage/Keystone-France/Gamma-Keystone/
posts.alt_text: Photo by CNN Style on May 21, 2026. May be a Twitter screenshot of text that says 'What better way to honor one of fashion history's most famously spontaneous moments than with an elaborate custom gown that took 130 artisans a combined 22,160 hours to produce?'.

posts.caption:
On Monday evening, it took auction house @christiesinc just 40 minutes to sell more than $630 million in art, smashing records for both the painter Jackson Pollock and sculptor Constantin Brancusi. Later in the night, it rounded things off with yet more broken records and another $490 million.
The first part of the $1.1-billion New York sale featured 16 works from S.I. Newhouse's collection. It marked the fourth time Christie's has brought a group of works to market from the late media giant, who owned @condenast, as well as large slice of America's local newspapers and broadcast stations.
But it was the first time that the auction house tapped @nicolekidman for a promotional video, filming her free-flowing encounter with the golden Brancusi bust "Danaïde," which was poised to be one of the night's star lots. The video was based on a 1930s Man Ray film of fellow surrealist Lee Miller, per Christie's, and while it didn't quite take off in the same way as Kidman's viral AMC ad, it did once again prove her commitment to any and all roles.
Monday's sales at Christie's also included three masterworks from the collection of former Museum of Modern Art president Agnes Gund, which set a new $98.4 million record for Mark Rothko, while Sotheby's offered up treasures worth $166.3 million from the late art dealer Robert Mnuchin last week.
📸 : Hunter Abrams/Courtesy Christie's
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on May 19, 2026 tagging @christiesinc, and @nicolekidman. May be a graphic of one or more people, people standing, poster, magazine and text that says 'CHRISTIE'S SELLS $1.1 BILLION IN ART IN ONE NIGHT - WITH A LITTLE HELP FROM NICOLE KIDMAN CNN'.

posts.caption: Major and independent art fairs convened in New York last week, coordinating their schedules for a gauntlet of contemporary and historical art that was met with a buzzy reception and strong sales despite economic uncertainty. Here’s what we saw at @friezeofficial, @tefaf, @newartdealers, @indepedent_hq and @154artfair that made us slow down and take a beat during our speed run through the fairs — including works from @cindyshermanlegacyproject, @victoriaoniosun, @kellysinnapahmarystudio, @carrie_schneider, @ruthowensart, @rachelyoun and @katieryankatieryan

posts.caption:
On Sunday, @swatch issued a social media statement urging customers "not to rush to our stores in large numbers," citing concern for the safety of staff and shoppers after the launch of "Royal Pop," a series of pocket watches modeled on @audemarspiguet's iconic Royal Oak.
It added: "In some countries, queues of more than 50 people cannot be accepted, and sales may need to be paused." On its website, Swatch meanwhile assured customers that the collection will "remain available for several months."
Swatch's policy of limiting purchase to one per customer did little to deter scalpers. The items immediately flooded resale sites over the weekend, with prices on watch marketplace Chrono24 ranging from around $1,200 to almost $6,000 – a sharp increase of the original $400 – $420 price tag.
Videos shared online showed fights breaking out and security guards struggling to contain large crowds. A number of stores around the world closed due to safety concerns.
Like other "high-low" collaborations — from @stellamccartney working with @hm to @jw_anderson's @uniqlo collections — the pitch to customers is clear: A chance to own an exclusive brand that might otherwise be out of reach.
AP's former CEO François-Henri Bennahmias, once previously praised a similar collaboration between Omega x Swatch, which saw long lines forming outside Swatch boutiques in 2022.
A proud Swatch fan, Bennahmias said in an interview with Luxury Tribune that the Omega collaboration was a "great idea" that did not "affect the integrity of Omega at all … because it educates the younger generation about the icons of watchmaking."
Whether fights, mall frenzies and arrests change the calculation for AP is another matter altogether.
📸 : Swatch/Matthew Horwood/Getty Images
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on May 18, 2026 tagging @swatch, and @audemarspiguet. May be an image of wrist watch and text that says 'UIEIP ಶಿ SWATCH AND AUDEMARS PIGUET'S 'HIGH-LOW' 'HIGH COLLAB SPARKED A FRENZY. WHO'S THE REAL WINNER?'.

posts.caption:
This collection understood clothing as character.
David Koma Pre-Fall 2025 has that thing where the clothes don’t just look good, they immediately tell you who the person wearing them might be.
There’s a lot happening here: sheer bodysuits, fur collars, red tights, sharp heels, leather, denim, long black silhouettes, strange proportions. But it still feels controlled. Nothing looks random, even when the shapes are dramatic.
The collection plays with a very specific kind of glamour. Not soft, not romantic, not too clean. More like someone dressing up to be watched, but still keeping a bit of distance.
The fur pieces make the body look framed. The red looks feel almost graphic. The sheer pieces keep things light, but never weak. Even the denim and leather moments still belong to the same world.
That’s what makes it interesting. It’s not just a set of nice looks. It feels like a cast of characters.
Credits:
Collection: David Koma Pre-Fall 2025
Designer: David Koma

posts.caption:
The long tailored coat has always belonged to people who understand presence.
Before it became a fashion statement, it was a garment of function: protection, structure, authority. Military greatcoats, officer coats, riding coats, city overcoats, all of them were built around the same idea: length creates command. A short jacket moves with the body. A long coat changes the body.
That is the power of tailoring. It doesn’t simply cover; it edits. The shoulder is sharpened, the torso is lengthened, the walk becomes slower, more deliberate. A well-cut coat gives the wearer architecture before they even enter a room.
In fashion, the long coat has moved through many lives. It has been masculine, cinematic, severe, romantic, corporate, rebellious. On women, especially, it carries a particular tension: the language of menswear, reworked into something colder, softer, more dangerous. The best versions never feel decorative. They feel controlled.
What makes a long tailored coat good is not only the fabric, but the discipline behind it. The weight of the wool, the fall of the lapel, the placement of the button, the line from shoulder to hem. Too little structure and it becomes a robe. Too much and it becomes costume. The perfect coat sits somewhere in between: precise, but not stiff; dramatic, but not theatrical.
Today, its appeal feels even sharper. In a fashion landscape obsessed with novelty, the long tailored coat offers something rarer: permanence. It doesn’t rely on trend. It relies on proportion, fabric, and attitude.
A long coat does not complete an outfit.
It becomes the outfit.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 19, 2026. May be an image of duffle coat, overcoat, suit and text that says 'CULT selects: Long Tailored Coats. CULT'.

posts.caption:
Elizaveta Porodina approaches fashion photography less like a traditional editorial and more like a piece of visual theatre. In Carnival for Numéro Netherlands, fashion becomes surreal, emotional and slightly unsettling at the same time. The images move between fantasy and distortion, using exaggerated beauty looks, mirrored faces, sculptural silhouettes and dramatic lighting to create an atmosphere that feels closer to performance art than commercial fashion photography.
What makes Porodina’s work stand out is how immersive it feels. Every image looks completely constructed, yet never cold or overly digital. Her use of color, shadows and texture gives the photographs a painterly quality, almost like contemporary portraits inspired by cinema, surrealism and classical art all at once. Even the strangest details still feel human.
Maggie Maurer carries the editorial almost like a character moving through different emotional states rather than simply modeling clothes. One frame feels soft and ghostlike, the next intense or confrontational. The beauty work by Cécile Paravina plays a huge role in shaping that transformation. Bleached brows, graphic textures, metallic skin and distorted facial details push the makeup beyond beauty and into image making itself.
What makes Carnival so memorable is that it fully commits to its own visual language. It doesn’t try to feel wearable or commercially safe. Instead, it reminds people that fashion editorials can still be strange, emotional and imaginative without losing their impact.
Editorial Credits
Editorial: Carnival
Publication: Numéro Netherlands Issue 02
Photography: Elizaveta Porodina
Model: Maggie Maurer
Styling: Lisa Jarvis
Makeup: Cécile Paravina
Hair: Olivier Schawalder
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 18, 2026. May be a graphic of one or more people, makeup, hair, magazine and text that says 'The industry needs more editorials like this. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
Long before beauty tutorials and clean girl aesthetics, makeup was ritual, rebellion, theatre, status, protest. Ancient Egyptians painted their eyes almost like armor. In the 1920s, women used makeup as a symbol of freedom. And in fashion, the most interesting looks always came from people willing to look a little uncomfortable.
That’s why avant-garde makeup matters. The faceless skin, metallic bodies, distorted brows or surreal shapes aren’t supposed to feel safe, they’re supposed to make you stop and look. Fashion has always needed exaggeration, experimentation and a bit of madness to move forward. Otherwise everything starts looking the same.
Sometimes the best makeup look is the one that makes people ask: “wait… why does this make me feel weird?”
If you are featured in any of these images or are the makeup artist/creative behind one of the looks and would like to be credited, feel free to message us anytime so we can add the proper credits.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 17, 2026. May be a meme of one or more people, makeup, bust and text that says 'Normal makeup scares me. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
Feathers have always existed somewhere between fashion and fantasy. Long before modern couture, they were symbols of power, status and spectacle, worn in royal courts, ceremonial dress and later transformed into pure glamour by Hollywood and haute couture. By the 1920s, feathers had already become one of fashion’s most dramatic signatures, moving with the body in a way no other material really can.
What makes feathers so timeless is how easily they shift moods. They can feel elegant, dangerous, theatrical or completely surreal depending on the designer, the styling or the photograph itself. That’s probably why fashion photography keeps returning to them. Feathers don’t just add texture, they create movement, shadow and emotion. They make clothes feel alive.
Looking at these images, you can see how different that language can become. Some looks feel sharp and almost intimidating, especially the darker silhouettes and oversized black feathers that turn the models into these sculptural, unreal characters. Others feel softer and exaggerated in a more playful way, like the pale feathered dresses that almost float against the white background. Even the bright red look feels less like clothing and more like performance.
Fashion constantly reinvents feathers, but the reason they survive never changes: they make people look twice.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 16, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'Say what you want, Feathers matter. CULT'.

posts.caption:
For decades, diamond retail success in the US was driven by one formula: stock more, sell more, discount more.
That formula is changing.
Today, consumers are buying fewer pieces, but spending more on purchases that feel meaningful, personal, and worth the price.
With the US accounting for 53% of global diamond jewellery demand, this shift is sending a clear message to the entire industry.
What this means for jewellers, manufacturers, and exporters:
The winners will not be those with the largest inventory.
They will be the ones who can create stronger emotional value, build consumer trust, and position both natural and lab-grown diamonds with clarity.
Value over volume is the new rule of diamond retail.
Save this post for your next strategy discussion and share it with someone in the jewellery business.
📖 Read the full analysis in the latest issue of Diamond World.
#DiamondWorld #DiamondRetail #USDiamondMarket #B2BJewellery #DiWMagazine
posts.alt_text: Photo by Diamond World on May 21, 2026. May be an image of ring, magazine, poster and text that says 'DW DIAMONDWORLD How the US DIAMOND MARKET Is Rewriting THE RULES OF RETAIL What leading industry voices say say-and and what jewellers, manufacturers, and exporters must do next.'.

posts.caption:
For decades, diamond retail success was measured by one thing: volume.
More inventory.
More promotions.
More units sold.
That model is being rewritten.
The US, which accounts for 53% of global diamond jewellery demand — is shifting toward a value-driven retail model where consumers are buying fewer pieces but spending more per purchase.
Here’s what’s changing:
💎 Finished jewellery is outperforming loose diamonds
📈 Average ticket sizes are rising sharply
🔬 Lab-grown diamonds are dominating entry-level demand
✨ Natural diamonds are being repositioned as premium luxury
🛍 Storytelling, traceability and trust are driving conversions
The message for manufacturers, wholesalers and retailers is clear:
Success in 2026 won’t come from selling more.
It will come from selling smarter.
If your product offers stronger design, emotional significance and a compelling story, consumers are willing to pay for it.
What’s your biggest takeaway from this shift in US diamond retail?
#DiamondWorld #DiamondRetail #NaturalDiamonds #DWMagazine

posts.caption:
After years of soft neutrals, skin tints, and “no-makeup makeup” dominating the algorithm, Marc Jacobs Beauty is relaunching on its own terms with statement shades and party balloon-inspired packaging.
The brand first debuted in 2013 and quickly reached cult status before disappearing in 2021 after its partnership with Kendo ended. Now it returns under a new license with Coty Inc., re-entering a market that has fundamentally shifted.
While “clean girl” minimalism has dominated beauty since the brand shuttered, the cultural pendulum seems to be swinging towards color and maximalism, and away from former mega-brands like Glossier.
Marc Jacobs Beauty is returning with saturated pigments, high-gloss finishes, and sculptural packaging in a relaunch Jacobs calls a celebration of “creativity and self-expression.”
The rollout includes seven hero products priced from $26 to $42, reviving fan favorites like its best-selling gel liners in 21 shades, alongside new products like a multi-use blush stick.
Whether this return meaningfully disrupts the beauty category remains to be seen, but it’s clearly not trying to blend in.
Marc Jacobs Beauty drops May 28 on marcjacobs.com, followed by a June 1 release at Sephora.
#marcjacobs #beauty #makeup #marcjacobsbeauty #sephora

posts.caption:
On May 19th, Jonathan Andic was arrested in connection to the death of his father Isak Andic, the billionaire founder of Mango, who died in 2024 after slipping into a ravine on a Montserrat hiking trail. Authorities have outlined new evidence against Jonathan, prompting his arrest as a homicide suspect.
Jonathan, the only witness to his father’s death, claimed that he was a few meters ahead on the hiking trail when his father paused to take photos of the scenery. He told emergency services that he heard the fall, but did not see it occur. He also claimed that his relationship with his father was good, despite known tensions around Jonathan’s business failings at Mango in 2015.
However, new evidence shows that Isak’s cell phone was found in his pocket at the time of his death, and no photos appear on his camera roll of the hike. As well, the autopsy report found no scuffs on Isak’s hands, suggesting that he fell backward, not forward, into the ravine. Examination of Isak’s shoes also showed that the soles were worn down “deliberately” to create the appearance of a slip. Jonathan was also found to have visited the hiking trail multiple times shortly before his father’s death, causing investigators to suspect premeditation.
Most importantly, Jonathan’s tense relationship with his father was illuminated by Estefanía Knuth, the professional golfer and long-term girlfriend of Isak (who happens to be in a legal dispute with Isak’s children for €70m of the inheritance.) Knuth’s claims are supported by WhatsApp messages between Jonathan and Isak which reveal the son’s “obsession with money” and his demands for a living inheritance, which Isak was “forced” to agree to.
Finally, in 2024, Jonathan learned that Isak had planned to set up a “foundation to help people in need.” WhatsApp messages show a shift in the son’s attitude towards his father, causing him to express a desire to reconcile their differences. Thus, Jonathan invited Isak on the hike in Montserrat, just the two of them, to “talk alone.” Wanting to recover his relationship with his son, Isak accepted the invitation that would lead to his untimely death, and the foundation was never realized.

A photo of the White House is going viral for capturing the absolute chaos happening on America’s front lawn right now. Very on-brand for this administration, honestly.
“All of this paid for by myself,” Trump shouted to reporters over a gaping pit and the sound of heavy machinery, insisting the renovations are a “gift” to the country.
According to the president, the new ballroom is also a new security flex for additional construction underneath it. “The ballroom becomes a shield for everything that you see here,” he said, describing it as “the one opportunity for the military to do something.” Guess the bunker theories were right all along.
The president’s comments come as Republicans struggle to pass nearly $1B in funding for ballroom security and other WH projects, despite Trump repeatedly insisting his passion project would not be paid for by taxpayers (shocking, right?)
Can Congress stop this Home Depot-gilded mess before he gets any more ideas, ffs?!

posts.caption:
As AI looms over job prospects, it’s no surprise it has become an unwelcome topic at this year’s college commencements… well, at least for new grads anyway.
Two viral videos emerged over the past week showing graduation speakers gloating about the future of AI. Gloria Caulfield, a real estate exec, went viral after she looked visibly shocked as students booed her May 8 commencement speech at the University of Central Florida after comparing AI to the industrial revolution. “What happened?,” she asked. “I struck a nerve.”
That same energy popped up again at the University of Arizona, where former Google CEO Eric Schmidt delivered a similar message last Friday, but even more aggressively. “If you don’t care about science that’s okay,” he stated, “because AI is gonna touch everything else as well.” Schmidt reportedly anticipated backlash from students following allegations of rape and sexual harassment, and surveillance, filed in a lawsuit last November.
Online, the comments are basically echoing the same vibe. “Aren’t commencement addresses supposed to be motivating?,” one Threads user wrote. “Why do they keep sending AI simps to college graduations to brag to them about how AI is going to steal their jobs?,” another asked.
At Glendale Community College, dozens of graduate names were reportedly skipped and/or mispronounced by an AI reader replacing traditional announcers.
College president Dr. Tiffany Hernandez laughed off the tech issue, saying, “Each of you were able to walk the stage and get a picture, which is what I would hope is the most meaningful,” she said. “I am so sorry. There’s plenty of opportunities, I hope, to take some really good pictures and to celebrate you with your loved ones as well.”
Maybe AI just isn’t needed in a tradition that’s been doing fine for hundreds of years without it.
#graduation #2026 #jobs #news #reels

posts.caption:
Everlane has officially sold to Shein, the fast-fashion giant, in light of the company’s $90m debt burden.
The capsule-wardrobe retailer was founded in 2011, promising sustainability and “radical transparency” in their pricing and supply chain. Their target customer was a coming-of-age millennial who was eager for affordable fashion that aligned with their value systems. For years, Everlane remained the de facto workplace-casual retailer for that very customer, offering simple, non-branded, trend resistant options which supposedly didn’t come from sweatshops and took a bullheaded approach to cutting carbon emissions.
You could say that Everlane and brands like it defined the 2010s, to some extent. Everlane was a leader in a decade of optimistic retailers, each of which sold its own tailored promise of creating a better world through DTC sales; Reformation sold ethical occasion dresses, All Birds sold sneakers made of recycled materials, Outdoor Voices sold inclusive athleisure, and so on. The millennial utopia was, it seems, an ethical value proposition that consumers and investors could get behind.
But now, many of these brands are falling apart under the crushing truth of capitalism: Ethical consumption may not be as realistic on the large-scale as we’d hoped it could be. Everlane is, afterall, being acquired by the very force of retail that it was supposed to replace, one that would sooner churn out desirable styles and exploit consumers’ growing interest in hyper-trends and luxury than worry over the environment or supply chain problems like slave labour.
What does that mean for customers who relied on Everlane to provide a cohesive wardrobe sans effort, anxiety, or guilt? One Reddit post asks: “What are we buying/wearing to replace Everlane?” The responses are bleak, offering little in terms of replacements and much in terms of disappointment.
“I’m just so sick of everything. I just want quality clothing that’s not made from child labor and has ethical/sustainable production lines,” one user said.
Can a brand like that exist anymore? Or is the dream of the 2010s dead?

posts.caption:
What an incredible finish to the 2025-26 Premier League race (@premierleague). Arsenal (@arsenal) were crowned champions of England for the first time in 22 years after Manchester City's (@mancity) draw at Bournemouth on Tuesday night.
In a topsy turvy season, where Arsenal had the title in their sights, almost lost it, had it again, almost lost it again and then finally wrestled it back, Mikel Arteta's (@mikelarteta) men have finally brought the coveted trophy back to North London.
Manchester City face an uncertain future in the next few days. It remains to be seen if Pep Guardiola will finally bring the curtains down on his 10-year stay at the club. Arsenal, meanwhile, can dream of adding a sprinkling of European glory to their Premier League triumph.
#EsquireIndia #PremierLeague #Arsenal

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There is a version of Zinnia Kumar (now Rose) (@zinniakumar) that fashion understands easily. The model. An image that moves seamlessly between Paris, New York, and wherever the industry gathers next.
It is also the least interesting version of her.
We’re at Le Bristol in Paris and the day carries all the familiar markers of a luxury cover shoot. Controlled light. Precise framing. Cartier anchoring the narrative. But Rose doesn’t perform that script in the expected way. She arrives ready, slips into each look without negotiation, barely checks the monitor. There is no sense of overworking the image, only an instinctive understanding of how to move within it. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and green dyed agate, Clash de Cartier necklace in rose gold, Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and chalcedony, Clash De Cartier ring in rose gold and onyx, and Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and red dyed agate, all by @cartier. posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: Between takes, Zinnia Rose (@zinniakumar) speaks about working with domestic violence and coercive control survivors, particularly within South Asian communities. About helping women reclaim confidence, voice, and agency within legal systems that often fail to recognise the complexity of their experiences.
“I haven’t spoken about this publicly before, but I spent ten years in an abusive, financially and coercively controlling marriage,” she shares. “It shaped how much of myself I could express. Reclaiming myself and healing has been a long process, and one of the first things I did was remove the name he gave me. I’m returning to my birthright, as Zinnia Jothi Rose.”
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.

posts.caption:
#ELLEFashion: Shot against moody urban backdrops and washed in muted tones, GRAINE’s Summer 2026 campaign leans into the feeling of a city right after rainfall. The visuals play with reflection, texture, movement, and distortion, bringing together translucent fabrics, distressed surfaces, industrial detailing, and fluid silhouettes that feel both raw and wearable. The campaign captures the collection’s central idea of clothing as something that changes with time, environment, and movement rather than remaining pristine or fixed.
Titled 'Rain Graine,' the collection explores mixed media through distressed washouts, water-dipped treatments, layered textures, and irregular patterns inspired by rain hitting concrete. Traditional techniques like chikankari are reinterpreted in a more atmospheric way, while silk prints developed from original artworks by the founder are combined with yarn-dyed stripes, zardozi, and ari embroidery. The lineup includes faux-crop T-shirts, embroidered and distressed denims, and occasionwear dresses with softer tailoring and fluid construction. The palette stays grounded in rain-soaked urban tones, including concrete grey, vapour pink, desaturated teal, deep blues, debris yellow, and washed whites.
#ELLECampaigns
posts.alt_text: Photo by ELLE India on May 21, 2026. May be an image of poster and text that says 'ELLE'.

posts.caption:
#ELLECulture: It started, yet again, with Dean Di Laurentis from Off Campus. One cocky smile, one hockey-player smirk, and suddenly the internet was back in its favourite recurring crisis: falling helplessly in love with a fictional blond man. Tweets from fans have started pouring in. “How are things going wrong in my life again?” “I can’t believe I fell for a blond man.” “This is a humiliation ritual at this point.” Fictional blond men, somehow, continue to have society in a chokehold. Maybe it’s because they come in such specific archetypes. The arrogant blond rival with hidden emotional depth. The himbo with surprising vulnerability or the emotionally repressed royal even worse, animated menace with abandonment issues. Across films, anime, television, books, and internet fandoms, blond men have quietly become one of fiction’s most dangerous genres. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read more.
Words by: @16ektasinha
[Off Campus, Dean Di Laurentis, Stephan Kayln]

posts.caption:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online hate: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Incredible as it sounds, @theodysseymovie is only the second-most physically challenging of #CharlizeTheron’s (@charlizeafrica) movies this year. Much like her co-stars (and fellow ELLE Summer 2026 cover stars), the Oscar winner has a packed slate this year—thanks, in part to her active role in producing the projects she wants to see, even act in.
“I knew pretty early on that I didn’t want to just go and act and then go back to my trailer and wait for the next scene,” she tells ELLE. But it also took time to arrive at that realization: “Sometimes that was hard for me earlier in my career.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 Issue, Theron opens up about her work on #TheOdyssey, the extreme stunts that her recent roles have demanded of her, and the one rule she lives her life by. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
@lupitanyongo is “deeply honored” to play Helen of Troy in @theodysseymovie: “I mean, she is iconic. What more can I say?”
Evidently, not much. She’s careful not to divulge too many details about her character or the film as a whole, as director Christopher Nolan has a reputation for wanting to maintain a sense of mystery around his films. But Nyong’o says she doesn’t mind being tight-lipped. “I love it,” she says. “All you have to do is say nothing. I love being the holder of a secret that everybody wants to know.”
For ELLE’s 2026 Summer Issue, Nyong’o discusses the layers underneath “the face that launched a thousand ships,” working with the prolific director, reflects on the importance of going public her battle with fibroids: “I’m sounding the alarm for myself, because I have normalized my own pain for too long.”

posts.caption:
For @zendaya, working on @theodysseymovie helped her see a need for greater empathy. After all, portraying the goddess of war Athena—especially as a creative person putting out art against a tumultuous global landscape—will do that to you.
“There’s a real heaviness, and a real responsibility to that. How much we lean into dehumanizing other people—that breaks my heart, that’s something I can’t get down with,” she tells ELLE. “Because I just feel we have a tendency to not see other people as human beings worthy of safety, of joy, of the same things that we’re also worthy of.”
For ELLE’s 2026 Summer Issue, Zendaya opens up about her role in Christopher Nolan’s upcoming epic, why she was drawn to her role in ‘The Drama,’ and what it means to say goodbye to her 20s. Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
These days, @annehathaway can’t help but smile when she thinks about where she is now.
Despite working in the industry for over half her life, the sparkle hasn’t worn off. “She still has a sense of wonder,” says Michael Showalter, who directed Hathaway in ‘The Idea of You’ and the Colleen Hoover adaptation ‘Verity.’ “She doesn’t seem to have become jaded in spite of being so famous and being in the public eye and all that comes with it.” Hathaway maintains that sense of wonder by refusing to take her career for granted. “For example, like working with Chris [Nolan] for the third time, it doesn’t get old,” she says of @theodysseymovie director.
For ELLE’s 2026 Summer Issue, the ‘Odyssey’ star reflects on her third collaboration with the prolific auteur, what drew her to the role of Penelope, and how she’s grown from the negativity she’s received throughout her career. Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
It’s no exaggeration to say Christopher Nolan’s highly anticipated @theodysseymovie, out on July 17, had no small challenge set before it. Bringing Homer’s epic poem—an iconic work of literature in its own right—to the big screen would require...well, a Homeric effort. And so the Oscar-winning director pulled out all the stops, assembling a cast of A-list actors to fill out his vision.
But while #TheOdyssey might be so named for its mythological leading man, Nolan seems to have understood how profoundly Odysseus’s decade-long journey home from war is influenced by the women in his orbit. His decision to set out for the Trojan War in the first place—and his subsequent struggle to get home—is aided and challenged by a coterie of women played by iconic actresses (and ELLE’s Summer 2026 cover stars): @zendaya, @annehathaway, @lupitanyongo, and @charlizeafrica.
To that end, our cover stars each shared insights from their time on the set of ‘The Odyssey.’ See the exclusive photos and get the full inside look at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by ELLE Magazine on May 21, 2026 tagging @zendaya, and @theodysseymovie. May be an image of text that says 'An Exclusive Look at the Women of The Odyssey'.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:

In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠

Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! posts.caption:
A mantra to hold onto: don’t let shopping define who you are 🕊️⁠
⁠
Too often, meaning is measured in what you own and what you buy, an “it” product or the pressure to “get something” seen as a cultural communication tool. That’s not true 🙂‍↔️⁠
⁠
If you’re having trouble quitting “stuff”, here are a few tips the Good On You team abide by—⁠
⁠
🌅 Consider the future, for you, the object, and everything else around you. Is this item essential? How will it age with you? Understanding its place in your life is a key to figuring out a need versus a want.⁠
⁠
🧐 Keep an inventory of what you own, to avoid duplicative purchases and needless accumulation. Understanding what you have is helpful in saying no to things you already own, especially when you don’t have capacity.⁠
⁠
⏲️ Set limits for yourself, to ensure you’re only buying one thing a month or only spending a certain amount of money. Better yet? Challenge yourself to buy nothing and to save money, placing an emphasis on future opportunities that can improve your life beyond the “now” of a single purchase.⁠
⁠
💭 Understand your temptations, be they pressure from push notifications or influencers you follow or your friend group. This knowledge can help you manage environments that push you to needlessly shop.⁠
⁠
How do you avoid the pressure to shop? Share in the comments, then explore a story sharing advice on quitting over-shopping ↑⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #Fashion #Shopping #Sale #Zara⁠

posts.caption: First they weren’t in the “business of fashion” when clearly they are. Second they’re producing and selling at a global scale while claiming to “save our home planet” convincing consumers “don’t buy this jacket” while it continues to produce said jackets. I’m tired of Patagonia telling people not to buy the jacket. If they removed 1 SKU from their collection across all stores globally, that would have a profound impact. Being sustainable isn’t just about textiles it’s also the materials and logistics to transport globally. If they vertically integrated they wouldn’t be able to blame their failure to become sustainable on their factories. Their work in progress report was an admission that they are in fact NOT sustainable. Their words, not mine.

posts.caption:
Since the death of Caroline Flack in 2021, there’s been a lot of emphasis on welfare protocols and psychological support for anyone involved in reality TV. This week’s allegations concerning MAFS UK, however, highlight that even with good intentions, a show designed to shock and entertain will leave its contributors vulnerable.
Grazia’s features director @hattiecrisell is a longtime viewer of shows like these – but now wonders if it’s time we face their ethical problems and just switch off. ‘Designing protocols for a dangerous show is like giving passengers a bucket to bale out water when the boat has been designed with a hole,’ she writes. Tap the link in bio to read more.

posts.caption:
For the latest @LouisVuitton Cruise collection, #NicolasGhesquière turned his gaze to New York City – a fitting backdrop for a collection rooted in the rebellious energy of downtown culture. The focal point? A collaboration with the late artist #KeithHaring, whose iconic drawings appeared across Speedy bags, dresses and T-shirts alike.
Elsewhere, Ghesquière’s signature ‘80s-inflected silhouettes came into play: sharp biker jackets, acid-bright pops of colour, exaggerated prom dresses and Venetian clown-inspired looks. A particular highlight was a Louis Vuitton trunk once owned (and doodled on with a sharpie) by Haring himself, which served as the collection’s starting point, encapsulating the irreverent spirit of New York in the 1980s.

posts.caption:
Not too long ago, #ImranKhan defined a certain kind of millennial leading man: soft-spoken, self-aware and relatable. Now, over a decade later, the actor returns not just with a new project, Adhure Hum Adhure Tum, but with a sharper, more intentional approach to style.
From timeless tailoring to workwear-inspired essentials, Imran’s wardrobe is built around permanence over trends. For him, great style isn’t about excess. It’s about understanding proportion, reliability and clothes that actually last.
Presenting #GQWorkwearGuide, according to Imran Khan.
Tap the 🔗 in bio to master all 25 rules of acing your #office style

posts.caption:
#GQTake: A viral IPL clip involving #ArshdeepSingh referring to #TilakVarma as “andhera” has sparked a conversation far bigger than #cricket.
In sports locker rooms and male friend circles, ridicule is often disguised as affection, with appearance and skin tone becoming easy punchlines under the label of “banter.” But because it feels familiar, it often goes unquestioned.
At the 🔗 in bio Adarsh Soni (@adarshhsoni) explores how colourism among men continues to survive in everyday humour, and what that reveals about masculinity, grooming culture and modern Indian male identity
posts.alt_text: Photo by GQ India on May 20, 2026. May be a graphic of text that says 'Male spaces in India often operate through a vocabulary where affection arrives disguised as ridicule, emotional vulnerability remains tightly managed, and humiliation frequently functions as proof of intimacy GQ'.

posts.caption:
Fashion has no shortage of beautiful faces, but @alexconsani represents a new generation of supermodels—the ones not just walking the runway but creating platforms, building communities, and leading with personality. First gaining attention on TikTok, Consani is now one of the world’s most sought after models. “I’m having fun,” says Consani. “I’m doing it for all 50 of the other bitches that would love to be in that room.” For Bazaar’s Summer 2026 issue, the supermodel talks about trying her hand at acting, moving into her big-girl home, and entering her “experimental era.”
-

posts.caption: Once again, @chloessevigny takes us all to fashion school. After attending @louisvuitton's cruise 2027 show in New York, the actress and Bazaar cover star was spotted at the house’s after-party wearing a vintage dress that could only be described as a deep cut. The asymmetric green-and-black striped dress with a braided rope belt came courtesy of the little-known (at least to most outside of fashion) Italian brand Callaghan and was designed by @nicolasghesquiere BBLV-before Balenciaga and Louis Vuitton. Find all of the details at the link in bio. #regram: @chloessevigny

posts.caption:
As a true chameleon on the runway, @alexconsani is able to embody exactly what is required of her: French coquettishness at @Chanelofficial (“I don’t know many other shows where every single girl is so excited to do it”), sensual side-to-side sauntering at @TomFord (“I love Haider Ackermann. I mean, it’s sickening!”), or a ’90s pump down the runway at @Gucci. (“Demna is my sis! He casts his shows like movies. Genius!”) Consani can do it all. She takes on her role as model almost as the script of the day, asking: Who am I becoming today? The supermodel talks about what’s next in her career, the fashion industry, dating, and more at the link in our bio.
-

posts.caption:
@alexconsani got her start in modeling at age 12 with Slay, an agency that pioneered the representation and platforming of trans models and also represented actress @dominiquet.a.r.jackson and model Arisce Wanzer. “There were a lot of really beautiful moments and memories that came out of that,” Consani says. “For someone like me, it was kind of an escape. Being hyperfeminine and exploring what being a woman meant to me was, at first, really a fashion experience,” she explained. At the link in our bio, read more about her rise to becoming one of the world’s most famous supermodels and what’s next for our cover star.
-

posts.caption:
If you’ve come across one of @alexconsani’s viral videos on your For You Page, then you’re familiar with the infectious energy she brings. From the small café in East Williamsburg where our interview took place to the high-profile runways she graces so regularly, she fills every space she enters with joy. “There is something immediate about [Alex’s] presence,” said @tomford creative director Haider Ackermann (@h.a). “The moment she enters a room, the world seems to lean in and embrace her energy.” Click to read the full cover story.

posts.caption:
Michael Rider’s @celine feels less like a total reset and more like a shift in attitude. ⁠
⁠
The biggest change comes through the styling: relaxed tailoring, layered knits, broken-in leather, ties worn slightly undone. Everything looks expensive without trying too hard. There’s a softer, more lived-in feel to the collection that trades sharp Parisian chic for a kind of refined American ease, making Rider’s Ralph Lauren past hard to ignore.
📸 @easternspy
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on May 21, 2026 tagging @highsnobiety. May be an image of overcoat, suit, belt, neckwear and text that says 'CELINE'S STYLING IS SUBTLE BUT IMPOSSIBLE ΤΟ MISS 브'.

posts.caption:
Bad Bunny’s @zara appearances suddenly explain everything. ⁠
⁠
After stepping out in custom Zara looks for both the Super Bowl halftime show and this year’s Met Gala, the artist’s next move felt inevitable, and now the “Benito Antonio” line is officially on the way. Puerto Rico got the first taste through an exclusive Plaza Las Américas pop-up in San Juan, complete with a surprise Bad Bunny appearance that turned the whole thing into a mini cultural event.⁠
⁠
The collection feels very Benito: oversized tailoring, relaxed streetwear, “NUEVAYoL” hoodies, baseball caps, and elevated staples that look pulled straight from his own closet. It’s polished without trying too hard, which is probably why people are already preparing their wallets for impact. The global drop hits Zara online May 21, and honestly, this feels like one of those collections that disappears five minutes after launch.⁠
⁠
Do you think it's worth the hype? ⁠
⁠
📸 @elconciertologo
posts.alt_text: Photo by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on May 21, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'FETTEL MNg FIRST LOOK FIRSTLOOKAT AT BAD BUNNY'S S ZARA COLLECTION'.

posts.caption:
We all see @stoneisland around the pitch but what about on the pitch? Or off-pitch, for that matter? Stone Island's latest and greatest New Balance kit is for the guys en route to the football match, whether they're about to get into the game or watch it with pint in hand.⁠
⁠
Revealed here by Highsnobiety, Brazilian wonderkid Endrick and Arsenal winger Bukayo Saka model Stone Island's latest New Balance collaboration, a genius off-pitch set that comprises some killer warm-up gear: a ripstop cotton trackie, fleece pullover designed to recall drill tops, and the primo drip of a nylon jacket layered with a transparent film that earns the title of "liquid."⁠
⁠
The accompanying sneakers are no less suave, with a tasty set of New Balance Abzorb 1890 decked out with reflective "teardrop" decorations affixed through high-frequency welding. Now that's liquid football.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by HIGHSNOBIETY on May 20, 2026 tagging @highsnobietystyle. May be an image of parka, magazine, jacket, raincoat and text that says 'SAKA MAKES LIQUID STONE ISLAND NEW BALANCE LOOK ICE COLD H'.

posts.caption:
501 Day always hits, but @levis Vintage Clothing really went deep in the archive this year with the return of the 1870 Nevada reproduction. What makes this pair special is how clearly you can see the DNA of the modern 501 taking shape, from the utilitarian left-thigh tool pocket to all the tiny construction details that defined early American workwear.⁠
⁠
LVC rebuilt the jeans with obsessive period accuracy, including single-needle detailing, 1873 rivets, leather washers, and deadstock Cone Mills White Oak selvedge denim made in the USA. It feels less like a regular denim drop and more like owning a preserved fragment of workwear history. The 1870 Nevada Jeans are available now exclusively through Levi’s for $595 USD.

@gentlemonster just unveiled its highly anticipated intelligent eyewear, designed in partnership with Google and Samsung. 

First announced last year, this collaboration builds on a wave of fashion-meets-AR releases that have been testing the acceptance of wearable tech in the last few years. 

While smart glasses are slowly gaining traction in creative spaces, particularly for capturing moments at fashion week and festivals, they have yet to achieve the widespread adoption the industry once envisioned.

That said, if anyone can make smart glasses work, it’s the minds behind Gentle Monster’s endlessly futuristic and technology-driven world.

What do you think? Will Gentle Monster get the smart glasses market moving, or do you not vibe with the vision?

Credit: Gentle Monster / Hypebae

posts.caption:
What are those ⁉️🛸 Futuristic footwear is what caught our attention at the @louisvuitton Cruise 2027 show, hosted at New York City’s historic Frick Collection gallery.
These alien-appropriate silhouettes resemble a hybrid between basketball and karate sneakers, combining the high-top build of a basketball upper with the flat-soled, laceless look of a martial arts shoe.
The shoes appear in a variety of metallic colors, including silver, gold, lavender, and blue, as well as solid leather colorways in black and white. Designed by Nicolas Ghesquière, the footwear collection also includes Keith Haring’s iconic artwork, also seen in the apparel line.
Stay tuned for the coming release of Louis Vuitton’s Cruise 2027 collection.
Photo: Louis Vuitton

posts.caption:
There are people who wear time, and then there are people who define it. @palaktiwarii belongs to the second kind.✨
She carries something rare, a weight that isn’t borrowed from the past, but isn’t chasing the future either. It simply exists, with the kind of quiet confidence that doesn’t need to announce itself.
In this frame, legacy and the now collapse into each other. @guesswatches on her wrist, intent in her eyes, highlighting the “Main Character” hunger behind the gold hardware; this is a story of a woman who isn’t just wearing the accessories of success, but is actively forging them. ⌚️
The Architecture of Time isn’t a concept. It’s a posture. A way of owning every second without letting a single one own you.
@palaktiwarii doesn’t follow the tempo. She sets it. And right now, she’s setting it for all of us. 🫶🏻

But off-screen things are less grim: Thurman is energized by her work and looking forward to the possibilities of her next chapter—one where she’s making space for new roles, new interests, and more time for herself: “I think the choice to be happy is one that should be made regardless of any conditions.”

Depending on who you ask, the word “manicure” has a different meaning. For some, it’s nothing more than an in-and-out polish change; for others, it’s understood as a multi-hour experience complete with ultraviolet (UV) lamps and hand-painted art executed with surgical precision. And then there are those for whom it’s exclusively about perfecting the nail shape and cuticles. As far as we’re concerned, it encompasses any attention paid to the hands and nails. That’s why we’ve collected the top polishes, tools, creams, and more that will leave your appendages feeling nurtured at the link in bio. #BestBeautyBuys

posts.caption:
This has been a blockbuster year for breakthroughs in hair care and styling. Once a category defined by utilitarian staples, hair care is getting more specific and sophisticated, with targeted treatments and styling products making salon-like results easier to achieve at home.
Additionally, people are paying just as much attention to their hair health as they do to their skin; scalp care is paramount, and searches for “hair growth treatments” are at an all-time high.
Our editors and experts tried hundreds—if not thousands—of tools, serums, shampoos, and sprays this year, and the 36 products at the link in bio stand out as being the best of the #BestBeautyBuys of the year.
No matter what your individual hair-care journey currently looks like, there’s something here to suit every texture, length, and preference.

posts.caption:
Makeup, like fashion, is a transformative tool for self-expression. Gone are the days of being boxed into the opposing camps of minimalist or maximalist. The best products on the market are buildable, blendable, and versatile—meeting us where we are day-to-day (and coming with us day-to-night).
In a highly saturated market, what differentiates a good formulation from truly a great one is ease of application. Creating a look you feel good about is infinitely simpler (and quite frankly, more fun) when you’re armed with the right products. At the link in bio, we’ve included a mix of the most innovative launches of late, like the Dior cream blush adored by the entire InStyle beauty team, and tried-and-true staples, like the Nars under-eye concealer one celebrity makeup artist @sophiasinot always comes back to. #BestBeautyBuys

Kritika Kamra (@kkamra) and Gaurav Kapur (@gauravkaps) have been household names for years. He’s the OG VJ-turned-sports anchor and host of Breakfast with Champions; she went from primetime TV to silver screens and streaming favourites. Lately, though, the internet can’t stop talking about their marriage.
“The first thing anybody says to me now is, ‘How’s married life?’ And I want to say, it’s all the same. Except now I live with my best friend, and I don’t have to decide what’s for lunch every day.” says Kamra.

posts.caption:
On 15th May, Drake (@champagnepapi) dropped three surprise albums: ‘Iceman’, ‘Habibti’ and ‘Maid of Honour’. In the days since, they’ve claimed the top 3 places on Spotify’s global debut albums, with 10 songs from Iceman on the top 10 debut singles and broken the record for the most streamed artist and album in 2026. The internet, however, is divided. While many are lauding the 3 hours of feature film long listening as the artist’s best music in 10 years, many believe it lacks the substance that a comeback at this point of the artist’s illustrious career deserves.
After hours of streaming, at the link in bio; Chirrag Guha (@guha.archie) reviews Drake’s triple-album drop, why he needs to get over Kendrick Lamar, and why he’s still not close Michael Jackson - yet.

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
The Audemars Piguet x Swatch Royal Pop became one of 2026’s biggest watch moments, but what should have been a celebration of design and accessibility quickly turned into a resale frenzy.
A $500 watch collaboration reselling for $1800+ within hours says a lot about demand. But it also raises bigger questions about brand perception.
For Swatch, hype-led launches are nothing new. But for Audemars Piguet, a brand built on heritage, craftsmanship, and carefully protected exclusivity, the optics are different.
When scarcity starts driving premiums instead of product value, the conversation shifts fast.
Smart hype creation or a risky move for luxury brand equity?
#Luxurious

posts.caption:
Jacob & Co. continues to blur the line between watchmaking, sculpture, and fantasy with the Astronomia “Gates of Olympus.”
Priced at $1.4 million, the one-of-one creation draws inspiration from Greek mythology, featuring Zeus, Olympus, celestial movement, and a fully mechanical miniature world built directly into the timepiece itself.
Inside the 18K rose gold case sits a triple-axis tourbillon, over 9 carats of baguette diamonds, and an intricate Olympus scene designed as part of the movement.
More than a watch, the piece reflects how modern haute horology is increasingly moving toward collectible art and immersive storytelling.
@JacobAndCo #JacobAndCo #Luxurious #Collab

posts.caption:
Porsche has unveiled the ultra-exclusive 911 Turbo S “Sadu Edition” to celebrate 70 years of the brand in Kuwait and the Middle East.
Limited to just 20 units for the Kuwait market, the special-edition 911 draws inspiration from Al Sadu, the traditional Bedouin weaving style recognised by UNESCO as part of Kuwait’s cultural heritage. Porsche translated the geometric textile patterns into bespoke interior fabrics, exterior decals, custom stitching, and handcrafted detailing developed through Porsche Exclusive Manufaktur.
Finished in Cremewhite with black accents and gold badging, the car combines cultural storytelling with Porsche’s latest 701hp Turbo S performance platform. Inside, the cabin features Bordeaux Red leather, custom Sadu-pattern seat inserts, illuminated anniversary door sills, and personalised accessories including matching keys and document holders.
The model also marks a major milestone for Porsche in the region. Kuwait became the first Middle Eastern country to import a Porsche sports car back in 1956, when a Porsche 356 Cabriolet arrived through Behbehani Motors, which still remains Porsche’s official importer in Kuwait today.
@Porsche @porschecentrekuwait #Luxurious
