posts.caption:
Mercedes-AMG has unveiled its most radical production car yet: the all-electric AMG GT 4-Door Coupe producing up to 1,153 horsepower.
Built on AMG’s dedicated high-performance EV architecture, the four-door grand tourer uses three advanced axial-flux electric motors paired with Formula 1-inspired battery technology developed through Mercedes’ motorsport expertise. The setup allows the car to sprint from 0–100 km/h in roughly 2 seconds, placing it among the fastest-accelerating production sedans ever built.
Unlike traditional electric sedans focused purely on efficiency, AMG engineered the car around emotional performance. The GT 4-Door Coupe introduces simulated V8 sound profiles, virtual gear shifts, torque-vectoring technology, rear-wheel steering, and active aerodynamics designed to recreate the engagement of combustion-era AMG models while delivering next-generation EV performance.
Visually, the design pushes AMG into a more futuristic direction with a low-slung fastback silhouette, illuminated grille elements, aggressive aero channels, and a highly digital interior focused on performance telemetry and immersive driving experiences.
The model also reflects a major turning point for Mercedes-AMG itself. For decades, the brand built its identity around handcrafted V8 engines and loud combustion performance. Now, with the electric GT 4-Door Coupe, AMG is attempting to translate that same character into the EV era while competing directly against cars like the Porsche Taycan Turbo GT, Tesla Model S Plaid, and Lucid Air Sapphire.
#Luxurious

In Manifest’s May-June cover interview, Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth) reflects on grief, love, and the kind of resilience that quietly reshapes a person over time. Beneath the poise is a life shaped by movement, loss, and emotional inheritance, experiences that continue to inform the way she understands connection and presence.
“You don’t know what life will throw at you. But as long as you’re there for each other, that’s what matters. It’s about support and presence more than anything else,” she shares.

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn’t seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest’s May–June cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
“I’m not very good at describing myself,” she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. “I don’t want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.”
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
On Wednesday, Louis Vuitton took over the Frick museum in New York to unveil a cruise collection that was nominally a meditation on the uptown-downtown divide and the way fashion travels between the two.
"Imagine a smashup of the Belle Époque glamour of a private mansion turned museum, filled with soft-focus oils of society doyennes past, and Keith Haring graffiti art with its democratization of the exquisite, and you’ll get the idea," Vanessa Friedman, chief fashion critic for The New York Times wrote in her review of the show.
On the runway, there was kitschy Americana (denim, cowskin and motorcycle gangs), faded denim and a bedazzled Chinese food takeout container, resulting in a chaotic mix of clothes and cultures, much like New York itself. Read @vvfriedman's full review of the @louisvuitton show and see more photos at the link in our bio. Photos by Gray Adam/ABACA, via Shutterstock, Arturo Holmes/Getty Images

posts.caption:
Frida Kahlo didn’t become one of the most famous figures of the 20th century by chance. The popular image of the artist that persists in Barbie dolls and on coffee mugs was a carefully constructed avatar — proof, according to the costume designer Jon Bausor, that “she was a living work of art.”
Kahlo had an awareness of the power of visuals from an early age: She chose clothing that concealed her disability but highlighted her heritage; she darkened her eyebrows, but refused to separate them. And whether posing for a photo or sitting for one of her many three-quarter view self-portraits, she was always aware of her angles. Kahlo’s personal presentation was almost an extension of her artistic practice.
A new exhibition at the Museum of Modern Art features works by Kahlo and her husband, Diego Rivera. But to better understand Kahlo as a painter, it helps to understand her as a performance artist, forever putting on a show for the world. From her plaster corsets to her spider monkeys, learn about all the things that made Frida Frida at the link in our bio. Photos by @rickyrhodes, Sylvia Salmi/Bettmann, via Getty Images, Bettmann/Getty Images, Banco de México Diego Rivera Frida Kahlo Museums Trust, Mexico, CDMX / Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York, @laurenfleishman, @interiorphotography

posts.caption:
There is a particular dread reserved for a wedding invitation with a complicated dress code. Garden formal. Coastal cocktail. Festive black tie. The request often feels less like guidance and more like a pop quiz, but for Jenna Lyons, it’s an invitation within the invitation.
Lyons, a former J. Crew president and a founder of LoveSeen, a fake-eyelash company, is, by her own description, a guest who gets excited about the assignment. In fact, the more specific the directive, the happier she is to play.
But she argues that being a well-dressed wedding guest is not about money or maximalism. It’s about creating a look that hangs well together: the dress, the shoes, the hair, the bag and the tailoring. And the fastest way to get it wrong, she said, is to treat a wedding like a workday.
As peak wedding season begins, the reporter @apbenven asked @jennalyonsnyc for her best tips for a great wedding guest look. Tap the link in our bio to read the full interview. Photo by @nataliekeyssar

posts.caption:
Drag queens, comedians and gay icons walked a hot pink carpet in Hollywood on Monday evening to celebrate the premiere of RuPaul’s new movie, “Stop! That! Train!”
In theaters June 12, the movie is a disaster film parody that follows two train stewardesses who lose their jobs on Stank Rail and start working on a fancy high-speed train called the Glamazonian Express. But when the Glamazonian is struck by lightning, it becomes a runaway train hurtling toward a severe and life-threatening “stormaganza.”
The premiere had a dress code of “choo-choo realness,” which some attendees took more literally than others. On the carpet, Betsey Johnson climbed out of a colorful luggage trunk; Symone, the winner of Season 13 of “Drag Race,” arrived with train tracks tied to her back; and the cast member Rachel Bloom wore a latex train conductor outfit. Get an inside look at the premiere and after-party at the link in our bio. Visuals by @alexwelshphoto

posts.caption:
The Cannes Film Festival is a stickler for rules. No naked dressing. No voluminous gowns or dresses with long trains. No sneakers. And lest they be barred from the red carpet, attendees should strictly wear black or navy suits.
Yet, year after year, the festival manages to eke out striking fashion moments. It is a testament to the creativity of the stars — and their coterie of stylists — who descend upon the French Riviera annually for the event.
This year, the festival is taking place from May 12 to 23, and it will celebrate films such as “Fatherland” by Pawel Pawlikowski and “Bitter Christmas” by Pedro Almodóvar. Although Cannes is seeing less of a Hollywood invasion this year, in part because fewer English-language films are premiering at the festival, the clothes are finding a way to break through.
At the link in our bio, see all of the looks (so far) from the Cannes Film Festival and check back throughout the week for even more photos. posts.caption:
Remember that viral #Loewe tomato bag? (Shoutout @conndo) Well, it seems #JonathanAnderson’s love for fruit and vegetables has left a lasting impression on Loewe’s designs. Their latest £4,000 leather artichoke bag makes you wonder, why are they so obsessed with fruit and vegetables?
These items aren’t just gimmicks; they’re demonstrations of Loewe’s craftsmanship, requiring intricate pattern cutting, moulding, and a high level of attention to detail. It also pays homage to the brand’s Spanish roots, drawing on a Mediterranean aesthetic of fresh produce and wellness. It aligns with a more relaxed, lifestyle-driven idea of luxury.
There’s also a sense of surrealism, where ordinary objects are elevated. That’s why these pieces feel more like wearable art than conventional accessories. Would you buy your 5 a day from @loewe ?

posts.caption:
When we think of archival fashion, the conversation is often dominated by the great Western maisons, but seeing #BellaHadid recently wear archival pieces from #ElieSaab, #ZuhairMurad, and #GeorgesChakra feels like a long-overdue reminder of how influential the work coming out of Lebanon was, and still is.
Lebanese designers really helped define the visual language of 2000s glamour at a time when much of fashion was moving toward minimalism. Their #HauteCouture embodied extravagance rooted in skills, passed down over generations across the Middle East.
(Images courtesy of Rani Fawaz/ Yasmine Diba / Bella Hadid / Elie Saab / Diggzy / Zuhair Murad / Getty / Backgrid )
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by umar on May 21, 2026 tagging @bellahadid. May be an image of gown, dress and text that says '主 No One Loves Lebanese Couture Like Bella Hadid'.

posts.caption:
@balenciaga’s new #ManoloBlahnik collaboration makes perfect sense for #PierpaoloPiccioli’s evolving vision for the house. It shows where #Balenciaga is headed after years defined by irony, provocation, and streetwear. Signalling to a sense of couture and refinement, we haven’t really seen in a while.
By collaborating with one of the most revered shoe designers, I love how Piccioli references the shared Spanish heritage of #CristóbalBalenciaga and @manoloblahnik which makes the partnership feel rooted in something deeper than celeb hype or marketing.
While theres still exaggerated sneakers for the fanbase established under #Demna, a pair of hand made crystal satin slingbacks seems to be the perfect welcome back for the customer searching for glamour and femininity once again.
What do you think of the collab?
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on May 19, 2026. May be an image of sandals, slingback shoes, magazine, high-heeled shoes, toecap shoes and text.

posts.caption:
Angels do exist, and they wear @i.migmoon, the 21-year-old Russian and South Korean student whose dresses i cant stop thinking about.  Inspired by his parents, who were ballet dancers, Ilya spent much of his childhood attending rehearsals, where he watched ballerinas warming up and practicing in delicate feathered tutus.
That early exposure to the world of ballet continues to shape his work today. He loves using wings and feathers explaining how he wants to elevate women into divine heroines like an angel or superhero. #Couture #IlyaMigmoon

Justin Bieber for Issue 01: “The First Ever” Outlander Magazine In-Print!
This is a real piece of history for us all. The past 10 years of lessons and learning have led up to this moment and I couldn’t be happier to share it with you all.
Some of you may be new followers who discovered us last week and some of you may have been here since day 1, either way this is for everyone who aligns with what it means to be an outlander.
Also, this is a great time to introduce myself for the first time in 10 years. My name is @callum.mccaff & we are all Outlander. I was born in 1996 so we priced the magazine at £19.96.

posts.caption:
Travis Scott for Issue 01: “The First Ever” Outlander Magazine In-Print!
GANG, TRAVIS SCOTT IN HERMÈS FOR OUR FIRST EVER MAGAZINE COVER. THIS IS UNHEARD OF, AND BETTER YET? IT WAS FOR OUTLANDER MAGAZINE. I AM HUMBLED.
WOW, what a moment. My only wish in this life is that what I’ve spent the last 10 years building acts as a strong form of inspiration for your own creative careers - I came from no fashion background, I had no connections & I had no idea what I was doing, but, I did it anyway.
If you didn’t know by now, my name is @callum.mccaff , I founded Outlander - and I’m here to remind everyone that PRINT ISN’T DEAD.

Leading clinical scientist Dr Soumya Swaminathan was elected as a Fellow of the Royal Society, the world's oldest scientific academy in continuous existence. She is only the second Indian woman to be elected in the over 400-year history of the Society.
With this honour, Dr Swaminathan has entered the coveted league of scientists in the order of Isaac Newton. Her father, late scientist MS Swaminathan, was also a Fellow of the Royal Society.

Taiwanese author Yàng Shuãng-zi and translator Lin King won the International Booker Prize for "Taiwan Travelogue," a romance set in Japan-occupied Taiwan in the 1930s.
They are the first Taiwanese author and translator to win the International Booker. The book is also the first originally published in Mandarin language to win this literary award.
Yàng told the Booker jury that she "wanted to untangle the complex circumstances" of Taiwan's time as a Japanese colony. Natasha Brown, the chair of the judging panel, said, "It's a captivating, slyly sophisticated novel."
Visit www.shethepeople.tv for more.

posts.caption:
Squadron Leader Saanya has become the first woman officer to earn a Category-A Qualified Flying Instructor (QFI) qualification. It is the highest instructional rating awarded to military aviators, and marks them as an exceptionally skilled pilot who is certified to train and evaluate other military pilots and instructors.
Cat-A QFIs undergo a 22-week course at the Flying Instructors School (FIS) at the Air Force Station, Chennai. The rigorous training involves over 200 hours of ground-based courses and extensive flying instruction phases.
Commissioned in June 2015, Sqn Ldr Saanya belongs to the 42 Short Service Commission Course (Women) for pilots on the Flying Branch which had six women. She was promoted to the rank of Squadron Leader in June 2021.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv for more.

posts.caption:
Some names don’t need an introduction.
They need a moment of pause… because what they’ve built speaks before any words can. ✨
Anita Dongre is one of those names.
Beyond the trends, the seasons, and the noise of fast fashion… there’s a quiet philosophy running through every piece she creates. A belief that Indian craftsmanship deserves to be celebrated, not just on occasion, but every single day.
And as bridal fashion evolves, her language remains distinct… no longer confined to just the grand lehenga, but thoughtfully expressed through silhouettes that feel rooted in heritage yet completely at ease in the present.
From hand-block printed anarkalis that feel like they were made for open courtyards and golden light, to bridal lehengas layered with delicate floral embroidery that could only have come from a love of this land, to resort wear that carries the soul of Rajasthan into every thread… each piece tells you exactly where it comes from.
Some of her work is soft and pastoral, drawing from nature the way only someone who truly listens to it can. Others are regal and considered… rich in detail, yet never heavy in spirit.
Think of Anita Dongre not just as a label… but as a point of view. One that has consistently chosen the artisan, the craft, and the woman who wants to wear something that means something.
Because long after the outfit has been carefully folded away… it’s the feeling of wearing something so deeply considered that stays with you. The quiet pride of knowing the hands behind it, and the story woven into every stitch. A reminder that true luxury was never about volume - it was always about meaning. 💫
Which Anita Dongre piece speaks to you? ✨
Disclaimer: These visuals are for inspiration purposes only and do not belong to us.
#JustSpreehIt

posts.caption:
There is a kind of planning that you can see.
And then there is the kind you can only feel - where the transitions happen before you notice them, where the design and the logistics speak the same language, and where the guest experience is so thoughtfully held that nobody stops to wonder how it all came together. They simply enjoy that it did.
That is the kind of wedding Gagan and Sakshi had.
And then there was them. ❤️
But what made this celebration truly stand apart was how the precision never announced itself - the scale landed without overwhelming, every function moved into the next with an ease that felt natural rather than rehearsed, and the larger vision held firm from the first moment to the last. A celebration where every detail knew its place and played its part.
Some weddings are well executed.
And some stay with you because the planning was so complete, so quietly considered, that all you were left with was the feeling of a celebration that simply worked - beautifully, seamlessly, and entirely in service of the two people at the centre of it all. 🌟

posts.caption:
There’s a kind of jewellery that doesn’t try to outshine the moment.
It simply becomes part of it… softly, and without asking for permission. ✨
Pearlcore is one of those details.
Beyond the sparkle of diamonds, the weight of kundan, and the loudness of statement jewellery… there’s something almost romantic about a pearl. A detail that doesn’t demand attention, yet somehow holds it completely.
And as weddings evolve, so does the way brides choose to adorn themselves… no longer just reaching for the heaviest heirloom pieces, but thoughtfully layering details that feel luminous, considered, and quietly luxurious.
From pearl-draped maang tikas that feel almost ethereal, to delicate pearl borders on dupattas that catch the light with every step, to strands woven into hair, embroidered onto blouses, and threaded through jewellery… each detail brings its own gentle glow into the celebration.
Some are singular and subtle, letting one perfectly placed pearl do what a dozen stones cannot. Others are layered and immersive… building a softness into the bridal look that feels completely intentional.
Think of pearlcore detailing as more than just a trend… it’s a mood. The quiet, luminous kind that photographs like a dream and feels like poetry in person.
Because long after the celebrations have ended… it’s the details that felt tender and timeless that linger in every frame. The ones you didn’t plan around, but couldn’t imagine the look without. The ones that caught the light at exactly the right moment, in exactly the right photograph, and made everything feel complete.
Pearls have always carried that quiet magic. And this season, brides are finally letting them lead. 💫
Are you team pearlcore this season? ✨

posts.caption:
There was something about the light that day - the way it fell soft and unhurried across the venue, the colours of the florals holding their own against the richness of the bridal ensemble, and traditions unfolding in a way that felt beautifully personal rather than performed.
And then there was them. ❤️
But what truly made these moments special were the people surrounding them. The warmth in every glance, the affectionate chaos of family stepping in between rituals, the happiness that seemed to ripple through every corner of the celebration… it all felt less like an event and more like a memory being created in real time.
Some weddings impress you with scale.
And some stay with you because of how deeply they make you feel - wrapped in honest moments, quiet intimacy, laughter that needed no occasion, and love so genuine it almost spills out of the photographs themselves. 🌟

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

posts.caption:
Jonathan Anderson just showed his first couture collection for Dior. If you do not understand why it mattered, this post is for you.
John Galliano was front row. The artisans were the opening act. The silhouettes came from ceramicist Magdalene Odundo.
If you are in Paris: the show space at Musée Rodin opens to the public from 28 January for one week. The new couture collection, archival Dior pieces, and the Odundo ceramics that inspired it. You can see it yourself.
This is not about liking or not liking a collection. This is about understanding what you are looking at.
The Studio of Stylist Elixir is our online membership. Guides. Templates. Live calls. A community of stylists who are learning and building together. Comment ‘studio’ for a free trial.
Save this. Share it with someone who needs it.
#dior #jonathananderson #stylistelixir

posts.caption:
A fashion stylist leaves school with one question. Now what?
The degree taught you the theory. The history. The construction. The language. What it did not teach you was how to find clients, set rates, build contacts, write a contract, manage returns, or recover from a job that went wrong. That curriculum starts on the first day of the working career and nobody hands you a syllabus.
This is a hands-on job, and the real learning happens on set. Assisting is where you earn it. Watching how a working stylist reads a room, builds a rail, handles a client, saves a shoot at four o’clock. The years spent assisting are not a detour before the career. They are the career, learning itself.
The portfolio you leave with is not the portfolio that gets you booked. The business side is on you to figure out, the same way every working stylist before you did, by getting it wrong first.
✦ You do not leave school a stylist. You become one with experience
The version of you five years into the job is doing work the student version could not have imagined. That is the only reassurance the working career will give you.
💚 For every fashion stylist who has just left school and is asking now what
💚 For every stylist three years in still figuring out the business side
💚 For every working fashion stylist who is the proof the course was the start, not the career
▸ Comment STYLIST to learn how we can help.
With love, Em x
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on May 21, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine and text that says 'I Went to Fashion School. Now What? STYLISTELIXIR LIXIR STYLIST'.

posts.caption:
A fashion stylist learns the unspoken rules of this industry job by job.
Never return a piece late. Never poach a peer’s client. Never give the full vision away in the pitch. Never complain about the client on set. Never be the one who makes the talent wait. Never say you can do something if you cannot. Always leave the set better
than you found it.
Nobody hands you the rules. You learn them by breaking them, or by watching someone else break them and pay the price.
✦ The career compounds on the rules you follow. The career falls apart on the ones
you break. ✦
Anyone who has been in this industry five years knows every line on this list. Anyone who has been in it ten years has stories about every single one.
💚 For every fashion stylist learning the rules in real time
💚 For every stylist who has watched a peer break one and felt the room change
💚 For every working fashion stylist who has built a career on the unspoken ones
▸ Comment STYLIST to learn how we can help.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on May 19, 2026. May be a graphic of poster, magazine and text that says 'The Unspoken Rules of Being a Fashion Stylist. STYLISTELIXIR LIXIR STYLIST'.

posts.caption:
A fashion stylist hears the same sentences from clients every single week.
Can you just. Can you do this for exposure. The talent is paying out of pocket, can you lower your rate. Can you get this piece by Friday. We want it to look expensive but natural. Do you have a deck yet. Can you replicate this. Can we do this for a favour.
The conversation is the same on every job. The work is to figure out which version of the conversation needs a yes, a no, or a polite redirect.
✦ The stylist is the one in the room who has to translate vague into specific, every single time. ✦
Anyone who has been in this job a year knows the words before the email opens.
💚 For every fashion stylist tired of “can you just”
💚 For every stylist who has been asked for exposure rates one too many times
💚 For every working fashion stylist learning when to push back and when to make it happen
▸ Comment STYLIST to learn how we can help.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on May 18, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine and text that says 'Things Fashion Stylists Hear Every Single Week. STYLISTELIXIR LIXIR STYLIST'.

posts.caption:
A fashion stylist learns most of this career the hard way.
The first five years are low pay, sometimes no pay. You will carry samples in the rain more times than you can count. Your friends might not understand what you do.
People think your life is glamorous when it is not. The work is lonely. You will be the only one who sees the faults in every image.
The rate conversation never gets easier. And the career compounds in a way you only see when you turn around.
Nobody hands you the manual. You learn the job by living it.
✦ The career is real. It just looks nothing like what you imagined when you started. ✦
Anyone three years in knows what this is. Anyone ten years in has lived every line on this list.
💚 For every fashion stylist still in their first five years, deciding whether to keep going
💚 For every stylist who looked at the rain and the bag and almost left
💚 For every working fashion stylist who stayed long enough to see the career start compounding
▸ Comment STYLIST to learn how we can help.
With love, Em x

posts.caption:
Collagen powder has slipped from supplement jars into smoothie bowls and beauty cabinets, sold as shortcuts to glow. But hype does not equal efficacy.
Experts point out that collagen does not rebuild skin. It provides building blocks and signals that can support collagen production.
Marine, bovine, and vegan collagen each come with trade-offs, but transparency and consistency matter more than marketing. Visible results require eight to twelve weeks of disciplined use.
Read more at the link in bio
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)
#collagen #skincare #beautytrends #wellnessindia #supplements

posts.caption: In most animal species, nature tends to maintain a roughly equal number of males and females because this balance maximizes reproductive success over generations. However, in many situations, populations may become female-biased because females are more important for increasing population size; a single male can reproduce with multiple females, but each female can produce only a limited number of offspring. In addition, males in many species face higher mortality due to fighting, competition, risk-taking, and greater visibility to predators. Some animals, such as bees and ants, naturally produce overwhelmingly female populations because only a few males are needed for reproduction. Thus, evolution does not intentionally “favor” females, but biological and environmental pressures often make female-heavy populations more advantageous for survival and recovery.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Established on May 18, 2026 tagging @drpoonamdesai, and @drkiransays. May be a graphic of one or more people, poster, magazine and text that says 'HEALTH & WELLNESS MEN AGE SLOWER. WOMEN LIVE LONGER. DO THE MATH'.

posts.caption:
There is a point where jewellery stops behaving like ornament and starts asking to be read. New York-based label Eskae (@eskae.atelier) sits in that space, creating sculptural pieces that challenge the polite expectations of modern jewellery. “I was inspired to start ESKAE by a desire to create jewellery that holds meaning beyond adornment… ESKAE is a play on the suffix ‘esque’, suggesting the essence of something rather than a fixed definition. I wanted to build a brand that feels like a state of being rather than a label, something that invites interpretation,” says founder Drishti Verma (@drishtivermaa).
Born in India and now based in New York City, Verma works between two cultural registers. India’s layered traditions and New York’s global landscape have shaped her understanding of “form, meaning and universality and how intimate belief systems can be shared and understood across different contexts.” In her work, that duality appears through contrast: structure and fluidity, restraint and expression, silence and intensity.
Drawing from mythology, philosophy and psychology, Eskae is anchored in what Verma calls the “unspoken”: emotional states that exist before language. The result is jewellery that feels sculptural, intimate and alive.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Established on May 15, 2026 tagging @drishtivermaa, and @eskae.atelier. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, tinfoil and text that says 'PAP'.

posts.caption:
In India, guilt has rarely been just a feeling. It sits inside silence, duty, family expectation, internalised shame and the promise of karmic correction.
Now it has another life online. A post, a reshare, a hashtag or the right therapy-speak can start to look like social accountability. Sometimes it is care. Sometimes it is performance. Often, it is both.
Privilege still decides who gets to look away. Women and marginalised groups are often left carrying the heaviest moral burden, while Gen Z is learning to draw boundaries and inherit a new guilt through visibility, outrage and the pressure to post.
What guilt reveals is less about goodness and more about power.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang)
#GuiltInIndia #InternalisedGuilt #SocialAccountability #FamilyBoundaries #performativeactivism
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Established on May 15, 2026 tagging @rahulchittella, @parikshit.kashyap, @anuj_basak, @nidhi.sharan_, @dattani23, and @ping_aman. May be a graphic of poster and text that says 'စဝီနိ၀ O HEALTH & WELLNESS GUILT IN INDIA IS STUCK BETWEEN KARMA AND INSTAGRAM'.

posts.caption:
Favre Leuba’s revival begins where serious watchmaking often does: in the archive.
Founded in 1737, the maison’s record runs from frontier instruments to formal complications. In 1962, it launched the Bivouac, the first mechanical watch with an aneroid barometer. In 1968 came the Bathy, the first mechanical watch with a depth gauge. Altitude, depth and expedition were not decorative ideas for the brand. They were functions.
That is why its Watches and Wonders Geneva 2026 debut matters beyond the fair calendar. Less than two years after its 2024 revival, Favre Leuba entered the conversation with two watches drawn from its own past: the Harpoon Revival and the 1737 Triple Calendar.
The archive also sits close to high watchmaking. Favre Leuba’s name has appeared on dials alongside Patek Philippe, Zenith, IWC and Jaeger-LeCoultre, including the Calatrava by Patek Philippe. In 1948, the maison also acquired Bovet’s name and manufacturing facilities, producing high-precision chronographs under the Favre Leuba Bovet name.
For India, the story runs deeper. Favre Leuba became the first Swiss watch brand to enter the country in 1865, with its first Indian store opening in Bombay. A Zenith and Favre Leuba co-stamped pocket watch, gifted to Mahatma Gandhi by Jawaharlal Nehru, is still on display at the Gandhi National Museum in New Delhi.
Some revivals feel inevitable. Favre Leuba’s feels like the archive moving again.
@favreleuba
#PaidPartnership

posts.caption:
Illustrated journalism has always lived in the margins of serious news, despite doing some of its sharpest work.
In India, it has appeared as famine sketches, political cartoons and graphic testimony, often capturing what photographs or straight copy could not.
In 2026, that form received one of journalism’s most visible validations. Anand Radhakrishnan’s trAPPed, a Bloomberg graphic story on a digital arrest scam in India, won the Pulitzer Prize for Illustrated Reporting and Commentary, shared with journalists Suparna Sharma and Natalie Obiko Pearson.
The story follows Dr Ruchika Tandon, a Lucknow neurologist held under digital control for eight days by scammers using video calls, staged authority and sound effects. With few images to lean on, trAPPed turns fear, surveillance and psychological control into reportage.
In a media climate where India ranks 157 out of 180 on the World Press Freedom Index, the drawn frame feels less like a stylistic choice and more like a serious journalistic instrument.
Link in bio
By Arshia Dhar (@urseaa)
#illustratedjournalism #PulitzerPrize #visualreportage #Indianmedia #cultureandmedia
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Established on May 13, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine, poster and text that says 'Illustrated journalismi 1S not new. Anand Radhakrishnan's Pulitzer-winning trAPPed makes it harder to dismiss'.

posts.caption:
@realbarbarapalvin delivered one of the standout maternity style moments of the 2026 Cannes Film Festival, announcing her first pregnancy with @dylansprouse through a series of major red-carpet looks styled by @marc_eram.
From a custom powder-blue Miu Miu chiffon gown at Parallel Tales to a sleek ‘90s-inspired Karl Lagerfeld tuxedo shirt and dramatic black skirt for Karma, Palvin balanced softness with sharp tailoring throughout the week. She also embraced bold silhouettes in an oversized pink gown by Willy Chavarria and an ethereal off-the-shoulder Ella Mae look for Paper Tiger.
What really made the Cannes run memorable, though, was the energy between the couple. While Dylan kept things classic in black tuxedos, the two broke from the usual red carpet stiffness by laughing with photographers and leaning fully into the moment.
We love our girl.
#BarbaraPalvin #DylanSprouse #CannesFilmFestival #MaternityStyle #RedCarpetFashion

posts.caption:
This week, fashion felt like it was rewriting its own playbook in real time. On one side: spectacle. While Cannes Film Festival is taking over France, Demna turned Times Square into a chaotic “GucciCore” runway for Gucci, and Matthieu Blazy’s vision at Chanel already appears to be paying off commercially, helping push the house back to the center of the luxury conversation.
On the other: vulnerability and personal narrative. Anok Yai returned to the cover of British Vogue alongside her parents after a serious health battle, Rosie Huntington-Whiteley leaned further into quiet luxury through her heirloom-focused Mémoire project, and Bad Bunny stripped things back for a deeply personal Zara collaboration rooted in home and identity.
Caught up? For now.
#FashionNews #LuxuryFashion #Gucci #Chanel #Cannes

posts.caption:
It seems like the fashion community is never not pulling inspiration from the late Diana, Princess of Wales…and honestly, for good reason.
Diana mastered something fashion still struggles to balance today: polish without stiffness. She could move effortlessly between royal glamour and off-duty ease, making oversized collegiate sweatshirts, biker shorts, graphic tees, and chunky sneakers feel aspirational decades before “model off-duty” became an aesthetic category online.
But her evening wear is really where her influence became permanent. Diana pushed against the rigid expectations of royal dressing, trading conservative silhouettes for sleek gowns, asymmetry, bold color, embellishment, and a kind of confidence that completely changed how royal fashion was perceived globally.
What makes her style endure is that it never felt overly calculated. It felt human, modern, and emotionally intuitive—which is exactly why designers, stylists, and the internet continue referencing her over and over again.
#PrincessDiana #FashionHistory #RoyalStyle #StreetStyle #TheCitizensPoste

Go celebrate your nearest earth sign for good measure.

posts.caption: Warning to anyone wanting to buy with Vestiaire…. Don’t do it. Bought a LV purse but instantly questioned authenticity (literally as soon as I purchased it) went straight on chat about this. They kindly offered to verify it. The purse came to me and it definitely appeared fake to me…. No heat stamp inside, no serial number, very thin cheap dust bag. Hardware not typical of LV. Sent it off for verification. Waited a few days and today I get a notification to say it’s been verified and is authentic…. 🫢 I do not believe it for a second. Go on to chat to discuss, they say it’s got a serial number (seller also told me no serial number as well as when I looked myself) so I asked where the serial number is and could I have a picture… bearing in mind now they have it they are refusing to refund me and they are listing it to be resold. They’ve said they can’t send me a picture and can’t provide me where it is. Which all makes no sense to me whatsoever. I’ve reminded them that me asking for that serial number is me asking for that of my property. I don’t believe it’s real and therefore don’t wish to keep it but it is mine. Asked to speak to a manager and denied. I mean I may get a refund if it sells in 2yrs? What if it doesn’t sell at all? I imagine that I won’t see that listing and really they will do anything with it because they have it in their possession even though it’s mine?? Absolutely vile company. I am a small buyer I’ve not spent thousands. I know me not purchasing will make no difference really but I did want to warn others. I will never use them again, I’m going to make a claim on PayPal and leave a trust pilot review…. It’s a good job I guess that it wasn’t something worth hundreds or thousands. Very very bad company.

posts.caption:
Look at anyone’s online bio today, and you’ll find a grocery list of skills and accomplishments. No one’s just one thing.
On ‘The Nod’ team masthead itself you’ll see an audience growth manager who runs a bookstagram. Our social media editor at work goes home to make comedic reels. And our fashion writer, we’re convinced, should have her own YouTube channel. It’s as 2026 as it can be.
Of course, our cover girl Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl) represents everything we’re talking about—movie star, producer, serial entrepreneur, chatty podcaster. A multi-hyphenate in the true sense of the term.
As The Nod turns two, all this month we will feature an impressive array of all-rounders: a brand strategist with an alter ego, a BAFTA-nominated actor dabbling in prog rock, a hustler with more jobs than days in a week, an architect who’s a drag queen…
But are these side hustles a great work-life blend to save us all from burnout or just faux-inspirational tales designed as coping mechanism? Is it a way to ensure that we’re relevant in a chaotic time where job security is dismal, technology is throwing new realities our way, and the world is at war? Or is it our unsatiable curiosity that is leading us there? This May, join us as we explore ‘The Age of the Poly-Professional’.
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Nod on May 18, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine, poster and text that says 'We're a generation that for long was told that we can do anything, be anything. Finally, we're paying heed. Welcome to the age of universal polymathy, or, as we're calling it, the Age of the Poly-Professional.'.

posts.caption:
Growing up in Chennai as the youngest of three siblings, acting wasn’t part of the plan for model student Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl). During her gap year after college, the then 21-year-old dabbled in modelling and ended up at a film audition for kicks. “I thought if I do get it, I’ll do it for a year and then go back to studying,” she says. “The idea was to do it temporarily, never make it a career. I’m sure my teachers were disappointed when I became an actor. I think some of them still are,” she laughs.
We’re not the first to ask her what her alternative career would be, but this time she has an answer: “I’m doing it now,” she says with excitement. “I think this entrepreneurial journey is what I’ve always wanted to do,” says the serial entrepreneur, whose work straddles films, beauty, wellness, fashion, food, and sport, all at once.
For our second anniversary issue, @chloechou13 flew to Hyderabad to chat with our cover star. Full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
For our second anniversary issue, there was no looking beyond Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl). Two years ago, she almost quit acting when she was diagnosed with myositis, an autoimmune condition that causes muscle weakness, fatigue, and pain. It forced her to slow down and taught her a lesson at peak fame that she will never forget. “I was just so focused on running this race for which I don’t even know what the prize at the finish line was,” recalls the actor of the time when everything came to a grinding halt.
Since then, she’s renegotiated everything: her work ethic, priorities, even brand deals. “I realised all the checklists that I was so eager to tick off weren’t my own. They were checklists made for me by other people,” she says. Now at 39, she’s not only a top-billed actor but also an audacious producer, a serial entrepreneur, a fierce investor, and a chatty podcast host. At the link in bio, the multi-hyphenate talks to @chloechou13 about success, silent retreats, swimming, and what is likely to be her joyful next chapter.

posts.caption: First of all, the only thing HR and Off Campus have In common is hockey and romance and there is more than enough room for both stories to exist on television, the constant comparisons are getting tired (especially when the off campus books came out 4 years before HR). Second, why would they get dress coded in college? And what gate? What security? lol. Third, they are all collegiate level hockey players so of course they are going to be working out a ton?! Also so what if I want to see hot guys sweating and working out on my screen??? Like sue me! Fourth, neither Hannah or Garrett are philosophy or English majors, so not sure what your point is there? Also, they are in a philosophy class together in the book so I’m not sure what the producers have to do with this lol Also, what’s so wrong with majoring in liberal arts??? Fifth, can we not generalize the break up as Garrett just not listening to Hannah? It was a lot deeper than that…. AND FINALLY, I will not stand for Garrett being labeled as unintelligent! He is very emotionally intelligent (which I would say is even rarer to see for a male rom com lead), also just because someone struggles with one subjects doesn’t mean they are dumb….

posts.caption:
A new era of Marc Jacobs Beauty (@marcjacobsbeauty) is here, and we at ‘The Nod’ cannot contain our excitement.
When the brand first launched in 2013, everything came in sleek black packaging. Now, Coty Inc (@cotyinc)—which also creates Marc Jacobs’s fragrance line—is relaunching the brand with updated formulations and joyful, dopamine-doused packaging designed by Jacobs himself. The new product lineup includes seven products, some of which feature the brand’s signature charm motifs (a daisy, a star, and a heart), elevating everyday essentials to playful, collectable objects. But the one we can’t wait to get our hands on is the Born Star eyeshadow.
Read more at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
“You may call March the last month of the financial year, boohoo, but in 2026 I called it ‘Hannah Montana’ season. Miley Cyrus’s Disney show about an ordinary girl by day and a superstar by night recently completed 20 years, and the world woke up to the fandom again. There were sparkle-filled watch parties, karaoke nights with blonde wigs, and my favourite: a themed spin class at Bengaluru cycle studio Chakra Athletica (@chakraathletica).
That’s where I first met Tanya Shah (@tanyaxshah), our instructor for the Sunday morning session, bopping around in the hallway as ‘Rock Star’ blasted in the background.
At this point, I had no idea that Tanya isn’t just a ‘Hannah Montana’ fan, she’s actually living a double life herself. On Instagram, to Chakra’s 27k followers, she’s spin girl Tanya, who wears pastel-coloured twinsets, hosts Pop Princess versus Bass Baddie classes, and hands out star stickers to keep you motivated. But on LinkedIn, the less fun side of the internet, she’s Harshali Shah, a healthcare consultant who wears a simple black blouse in her profile photo.”
At the link in bio, @darshitaagoyal chats with the corporate girlie by day, spin instructor by evening about her double life.
Photographs and Videos: @lekharathnam
~
As The Nod turns two, all this month we will feature an impressive array of all-rounders: a brand strategist with an alter ego, a BAFTA-nominated actor dabbling in prog rock, a hustler with more jobs than days in a week, an architect who’s a drag queen, and more.
Join us as we explore ‘The Age of the Poly-Professional’.
~

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com

posts.caption:
Spinster was a legal term in England until 2005 😟
Not ancient history or a relic of the far off, distant past.
Women currently alive were referred to in official legal documents by a medieval textile job title that had been weaponised into an insult.
Because that’s what a spinster was originally. A job title for a woman who spins wool into yarn. A job that involved skilled labour and was essential to the entire pre-industrial economy.
Then unmarried women got pushed out of better-paid textile work (because they didn’t have husbands with trade connections) and into spinning. The lowest-status work that was left for them.
So the job title became a proxy for marital status and by the 17th century it was the legal term for an unmarried woman in official documents.
Full episode on The Culture of Cloth 🎧 Ep 3: The Dictionary Was Named After a Woman Weaver. Link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on May 18, 2026. May be an image of text that says ''The Goddess TheGoddessProject Project Spinster mas a job title. Then it became an insult. veronica veronicatucker tucker'.

posts.caption:
My married surname is Tucker. I’ve been using it for years without knowing what it meant.
Tucker comes from the Old English tucian which means to torment or beat cloth. A tucker was a cloth finisher: someone who took raw woven fabric off the loom and scoured it, beat it, trampled it in water until it became finished cloth ready to wear.
It was skilled physical labour that was essential to the entire textile trade.
The same job had three names depending on where you were in England. Fuller in the north. Walker in Scotland and the Midlands. Tucker in the West Country…Devon, Somerset, Wiltshire, Dorset, Cornwall.
My husband’s patrilineal family came from Plymouth and Cornwall.
The name came from exactly the place where it originated, where the cloth trade shaped the landscape and the language for centuries.
And Cornwall isn’t simply the far end of England, Kernow is one of the six Celtic nations with a textile tradition that predates the Norman conquest by centuries.
I am a textile and fashion designer and I married into the name of a cloth worker from a Celtic textile region without knowing any of this.
This beautiful man and his name found me 🫶🏼😭
Tucker is one surname and there are dozens more carrying the same secret and I’m going deep on more of them in the next episode of my podcast, The Culture of Cloth 🎧 Link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on May 17, 2026. May be an image of wool and text that says 'Turns out my married surname was an ancient textile job. veronica veronicatucker tucker'.

posts.caption:
“I don’t want to understand everyone; I want a world where I live and let others live. In New York City, I was staying in the middle of a huge building, surrounded by people of different races and religions, and there was no tension whatsoever. Sloterdijk even talks about how we need to learn a certain kind of discretion. That’s what bothers me — the idea that I have to understand your world. How am I supposed to understand your world when neither you understand it nor I understand my own? I’m very superficial in that regard. You have a neighbor — establish some kind of order, with basic respect, and don’t push yourself onto them, nor let them push themselves onto you. I take great pleasure in discretion. Maybe that comes with age.”
For the May Issue of Vogue Adria, we spoke with @SlavojZizeks, one of today’s most renowned philosophers, about the future of the left, the dangers facing modern society, one night stands, and revolution.

posts.caption:
Not long before her father died, @ArynaSabalenka hired @JasonStacy.coach as her fitness trainer. To Sabalenka, Stacy is, she says, “like a father.” The longest-serving member of a unit that spends 330 days of the year together, he’s been instrumental in her recovery from losses on and off the court. Following a transformative introduction to martial arts, Stacy has also taught Sabalenka some of the fundamental precepts of Zen Buddhism. These include Zanshin, a relaxed awareness; Mushin, mental clarity; and Tomaranu Kokoro, a spirit that is always moving.
For Vogue’s May 2026 digital cover, @robertjhaskell meets Aryna Sabalenka in Rome to talk winning, losing, grieving, learning, and never letting up.
Photographed by @stevenkleinstudio, Written by @robertjhaskell, Styled by @tonnegood, Hair by @sandyhullett, Makeup by @markcarrasquillo, Manicure by @nailsbyetsuko, Tailoring by @sara.on.set.tailor, Produced by @thatoneproduction, Shot on Location at @faenamiamibeach. posts.caption:
How can you take fabulous writing and fabulous design and weave them together to create something new? Check out ‘Taste’. With fold-out pages, cut-outs, foil embossing and hidden envelopes carrying tiny pieces of art, reading it is an experience in itself. Swipe to know more and head to the link in bio for a larger glimpse at the newly released book.
Words by: Thea Mulchandani (@thea.mulchandani)
#VogueCulture
posts.alt_text: Photo by VOGUE India on May 20, 2026. May be an illustration of map, card, book and text.

posts.caption:
Arissa Khan (@arissakhan) and Luqman Ebrahim’s wedding in Mumbai honoured their Afghani and Awadhi backgrounds across every detail, from the food and music to the textiles, jewellery and styling. The festivities began with a mehendi where Khan wore a playful mirror-work ensemble by Itrh (@itrhofficial). For the nikkah, which featured a live performance by the Sabri Brothers, accompanied by Sufi dancers, the couple worked closely with designer Rimple Narulah of Rimple & Harpreet (@rimpleandharpreet). The bride also opted for an heirloom necklace dating back to the 1800s, a piece with “extraordinary history and sentiment.” Head to the link in bio for a larger glimpse at their wedding.
Image credits: @eshantrajuphotography
#VogueWeddings
posts.alt_text: Photo by VOGUE India on May 20, 2026. May be an image of net, dress, wedding and text that says 'VOGUE E weddings'.

posts.caption:
The women’s world number one @ArynaSabalenka understands that tennis can be opera: full of triumph and despair, grace and slapstick, sin and atonement. The 28-year-old has been at the top of her sport for the last year and a half, with a deep bench of brand associations—including with Gucci, Nike, and Audemars Piguet—to show for it. But since her earliest days on the tour, she’s been a fiery on-court presence, known for her outbursts (she’s a world-class racket-smasher) and tense exchanges with officials. (She attributes much of this to being “very much a Taurus”).
Time and training have helped her to temper that fire—but it also has its uses. “This is part of my personality that can drive me crazy, but it can also drive me into that real fight mode and help me play with passion,” Sabalenka says. “It’s two sides of the medal.”
For Vogue’s May 2026 digital cover, @robertjhaskell meets Aryna Sabalenka in Rome to talk winning, losing, grieving, learning, and never letting up.
Photographed by @stevenkleinstudio,Written by @robertjhaskell, Styled by @tonnegood, Hair by @sandyhullett, Makeup by @markcarrasquillo, Manicure by @nailsbyetsuko, Tailoring by @sara.on.set.tailor, Produced by @thatoneproduction, Shot on Location at @faenamiamibeach.  Vogue, May digital cover 2026.

posts.caption: Léa Seydoux is keeping busy. The actor has two films in competition at the 2026 #Cannes Film Festival, and she’s using her fashion to draw a line between them. At the link in bio, Seydoux talks to Vogue about her Cannes fashion, best red carpet tip, and her friendship with Adèle Exarchopoulos. Photos: @jasamuller

posts.caption:
Saheem Ali’s (@saheemscene) “Romeo and Juliet,” starring Daniel Bravo Hernández (@danielbravoh) and @ramyalatiahaikens, will tell the story we all know, but set in a Verona located along the United States–Mexico border. As Ali points out, Shakespeare never specifies what began the families’ feud, “just that it’s violent.” Though he wanted a production that spoke to our current moment, Ali knew a racial or partisan us-versus-them interpretation would be obvious and dull. So his Romeo comes from a Spanish-speaking immigrant home, while Juliet is an all-American mixed-race teen, and both live on the same side of the wall. Learn more about the upcoming production at the link in our bio.
Photographed by @normanjeanroy, Written by @itsnumberjuan, Styled by @tonnegood, Menswear Editor @michael_philouze, Hair by @edwardlampley, Makeup by @markcarrasquillo, Tailoring by Tae Yoshida for @carol_ai_studio, Produced by boomproductions. Vogue, May 2026.

posts.caption: What do Pop and travel or uptown and downtown have in common? They all came together in @NicolasGhesquière’s imagination as he dreamed up his latest @louisvuitton collection. While suitcases have long been LV's whole thing, the one that inspired Ghesquière’s 2027 resort collection for the house is something else altogether. Learn more at the link in bio.

posts.caption: This season marked Sydney Fashion Week’s 30th anniversary, and the Resort 2027 collections have settled into a new routine, finding a new home and a refreshed look. Many of this season’s shows were anchored in and around the city’s Museum of Contemporary Art, which boasts a newly minted runway space on the ground floor. The most unifying trend on the runways actually came from a noticeable pattern in the casting: almost every brand, both established and emerging, featured a refreshing dash of mature models. Australia’s own muse, model Rachel Waller, was a season regular, walking in six shows. Check out the next generation of Australia’s independent designers at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on May 20, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'VOGUE RUNWAY WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW ABOUT THE AUSTRALIAN FASHION WEEK RESORT 2027 COLLECTIONS'.

posts.caption:
Mr. @GiambattistaValli announced today that he will acquire full ownership of his brand @GiambattistaValliParis from Artémis. This agreement marks the beginning of a new chapter in Valli’s creative journey, which he will now continue independently, in keeping with the spirit of freedom and independence that has always defined his work. More details at the link in bio.
Photo: Joseph Bishop
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Vogue Business on May 20, 2026 tagging @voguerunway. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, suit, magazine and text that says '.្ទ BREAKING Giambattista Valli Takes Full Ownership ofHis Brand'.

posts.caption: All across the city, from uptown at the Met to Dimes Square, the art/fashion divide—or lack thereof–is being debated, and put on display. At Null Object pieces from the fall 2026 @zoegustaviaannawhalen show have been reframed within a gallery context—meaning the garments read as objects when hung sculpturally. “Art,” Whalen said, “requires the viewer to create an emotional response or reaction, which I guess is similar to fashion.” Tap link in bio for a sneak peek of the show, which is on view through June 5, 2026.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on May 19, 2026. May be an image of bathtub, bust, mannequin and text.

posts.caption: Even before @Matthieu_Blazy’s debut @ChanelOfficial collection hit stores this March, the company had returned to growth. Chanel global CEO @LeenaNairhr talks to Vogue Business on Chanel-mania, the 2025 rebound and menswear. “Early indicators are very good; clients are excited, staff are excited. It’s great to see,” says Nair on the current performance since Blazy’s collection arrived in-store. @LaureGuilbault has the details at the link in bio.

posts.caption: From Prada’s most recent runway to Demna’s ode to the Tom Ford era at Gucci, today’s coveted sandals keep circling back to what the ’90s got right: sensual without trying too hard, practical enough for women constantly on the go, yet refined enough to define an entire look. From strappy heeled sandals to casual flip-flops, shop Vogue's edit of must-have sandals at the link in bio.

posts.caption: There’s a reason the plain white tee is the ultimate wardrobe essential—it’s versatile, chic without trying too hard, and completely timeless. For summer, the little white dress proves to be all of those things and more. A fashion blank canvas, if you will. With Memorial Day weekend right around the corner, head to the link in our bio to discover and shop 10 chic ways to wear it.

posts.caption: Few footwear styles capture the carefree spirit of summer quite like the flip-flop—long loved for its ease, versatility, and unfussy cool. And the brand best known for them? That would be @Havaianas, of course. On May 22, @IsabelMarant and Havaianas will unveil their inaugural summer collaboration: four flip-flop styles ranging from tie-dyed pairs to studded sandals. With Vogue First, Vogue Shopping readers will get exclusive access to shop the drop 48 hours before anyone else. Tap the link in our bio to shop.

✨ Creator, Actor, PHENOMENON! ✨ On the June-July cover of Wedding Vows, we present the trailblazing @mostlysane, Prajakta Koli!
From building a YouTube empire to being the lead for Netflix Original Mismatched, from being part of Michelle Obama’s Day Time Emmy winning docu-series to being UNDP India's First Youth Climate Champion, her journey is nothing short of extraordinary.
With her debut romance novel set to launch this year, along with her first Amazon Prime Original Andhera, Prajakta’s journey is a testament to the power of dreams, hard work, and staying true to oneself. She continues to inspire millions with her authenticity, relatability, and unstoppable spirit.

posts.caption:
@maudeapatow is ready now. Fresh off her return in the highly anticipated third season of #Euphoria, Apatow is stepping into a new chapter with the kind of clarity and control that only comes from growing up in real time—on-screen, off-screen, and alongside co-stars who have gone on to navigate intergalactic levels of fame and careers of their own. "Just thinking about us being little kids and having no idea what was in store to now… It's insane," she says.
In our May cover story, Apatow opens up about the roles that shaped her, the relationships that fulfilled her, the theater work that grounded her, and the rituals—travel, matcha, and all—that keep her moving forward. Read our full interview with the actress and director at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
@lilamoss is having the time of her life. At just 23, Moss has already graced the cover of British Vogue, starred in a @marcjacobs beauty campaign, and walked for @coperni, @versace, @miumiu, and @victoriassecret. But her path into fashion wasn’t always a given, despite having a supermodel mother. “I was always really interested in fashion,” she says. “I was always putting on my mom’s shoes and doing fake runways. But it was never something really that my mom wanted me to do.”
From her very first modeling opportunity, Moss knew she had found her lane. Now, she’s using her platform to advocate for causes close to her heart—from proudly wearing her type 1 diabetes tech in editorials to collaborating on the first-ever type 1 diabetic Barbie. See how Moss is carving her own path in our summer cover story at the link in our bio.

The best style moments debut in summer, and the trends taking shape ahead of the warmest season of the year only further prove that theory. If you ask us, the reason summer fashion trends reign supreme is the serotonin boost that warm weather brings to people’s lives.
To determine exactly which summer fashion trends will dominate the upcoming season, we focused on S/S '26 collections, cultural events like Love Story on FX and the upcoming World Cup, and more. posts.caption:

⁠
After captivating audiences as Villanelle in "Killing Eve," the actress is stepping into quieter, more complex territory with "The Death of Robin Hood." ⁠

What was once a fragmented landscape of small, founder‑led labels has rapidly evolved into one of the Middle East’s most dynamic consumer categories. ⁠

posts.caption:
@karenwazen is scaling her namesake label from an influencer eyewear line into a full lifestyle brand, and she is doing it on an increasingly international footing.
The Dubai-based entrepreneur opens her first United Arab Emirates flagship for @bykarenwazen Collection at Dubai Mall Friday, the most high profile marker of a growth plan that spans a multistore rollout in Egypt, a category push beyond eyewear into jewelry and accessories, and ambitions in the U.S. and Europe.
Read more at WWD.com — #linkinbio

posts.caption:
The last time Nicolas Ghesquière showed a @louisvuitton cruise collection in New York, in 2019, he shuttled the fashion pack out to John F. Kennedy International Airport to the newly reopened and refurbished TWA Flight Center there. It was literally taking them on a journey for a season that is meant to be all about winter travel.
But for cruise 2027, the artistic director of women’s collections — well known for showing the seasonal collections at stunning and historically significant locales — wanted something more intimate and, ironically, city based. So on Tuesday night he invited fashion followers to the @frickcollection in Uptown Manhattan. The Gilded Age masterpiece had long been on his wish list of locations and this time he succeeded, perhaps helped by Vuitton’s new three-year commitment to the museum, which includes sponsoring free Friday nights once a month and supporting the next three exhibitions. While fashion shows had been held in the museum’s fountain-filled courtyard, this was the first time ever one was allowed to be held in its actual galleries.
Read more about the show and see more of the collection at WWD.com — #linkinbio
Photos: @giovanni_giannoni_photo / WWD
posts.alt_text: Photo by WWD on May 21, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'WWD KEa AKKLN RUNWAY AY PARIS 202 2027 RESORT Louis Vuitton Cruise 2027: Craftsmanship Pops at the Frick'.

posts.caption:
For more than three decades, @Nigo’s creative influence has traveled from the backstreets of Harajuku to Paris runways. Now, “Nigo: From Japan With Love” at London’s Design Museum offers a look at the designer’s journey through more than 700 pieces from his personal archive. ⁠
⁠
From vintage Americana and Bape to collaborations, ceramics and Japanese tea culture, the exhibition traces the inspirations, collections and traditions that continue to shape his work today. The exhibition runs through Oct. 4.⁠
⁠
Read more inside WWD Weekend at the link in bio. ⁠
⁠
Report: @tianweizhang⁠
Photos: Courtesy of Luke Hayes
